segments.s170-g25: پس is a narrative connective ('then'). آگاهی here means 'news/information' and functions as the grammatical subject; آمد is the simple past 'came'. سوی is a preposition 'toward' and نیمروز is a temporal noun 'noon' — together 'came toward noon' (i.e., at about midday).
segments.s170-g26: به + نزدیک marks direction towards proximity of someone: به نزدیک = 'to/near'. سالار گیتی‌فروز is a titulary nominal phrase: سالار 'commander/chief' modifies the compound گیتی فروز ('world‑illuminating / bright for the world') describing the addressee/recipient of the news.
segments.s170-g27: که introduces a following clause ('for/that' / relative/explanatory). از شهر ایران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the city of Iran' with از = 'from' and شهر ایران = 'the city of Iran'. برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' as its complement.
segments.s170-g28: همی before a verb is a poetic auxiliary giving continuous/emphatic sense ('indeed/was then'); خاک تیره = 'dark dust' (خاک here 'dust'). برآمد is again 'rose' and به جوش literally 'to/into boiling' — a phrase meaning 'burst into a surge/boil'.
segments.s170-g29: پراگند here functions as a verbal/adjectival participle 'scattering/strewing' that attaches to the subject کاووس (Kavus). بر + یال خاک is a locative phrase 'upon the mane (with) dust' — i.e. 'scattering dust on his mane.' This is a compact, somewhat verbless poetic clause describing Kavus's action.
segments.s170-g30: همه جامه خسروی is the direct object 'all (his) royal garment(s)' (همه = entire, خسروی = royal). کرد is the past auxiliary 'made/did' and چاک functions as the result noun 'a tear/gash' — idiomatically 'he tore his royal garments' (کرد چاک = 'made [them] torn').
segments.s170-g31: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmatan). چو + بشنید = 'when he heard'. The following short sequence ز و is poetic contraction of 'from it/from him' (ز = 'from') and then رفت هوش is a frozen idiom: رفت 'went/vanished' + هوش 'awareness' = 'his senses left him' (i.e. he was stunned or fainted).
segments.s170-g32: ز زابل is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Zabul' (ز = from). به زاری برآمد خروش: به + زاری = 'with lamentation' or 'in lament', برآمد = 'arose', and خروش = 'a cry/clamor' — together 'a cry of lamentation arose from Zabul.'
segments.s170-g17: برفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb meaning ‘they went/they departed’ placed at the start (poetic word-order). با مویه is a manner phrase ‘with lamentation/weeping’ (با = ‘with’ + مویه = ‘lament’). ایرانیان, the subject, appears at the end (typical Persian inversion in poetry).
segments.s170-g18: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase ‘به + آن’ (poetic/archaic) meaning ‘in/into that’ or ‘to that’; here it introduces the particular mourning. سوگ is the noun ‘mourning’. بسته is a past-participle/adjective ‘bound/tied’ describing people as having taken on that mourning. به زاری میان literally ‘into/among lamentation’ (به = toward/into, زاری = lamentation, میان = midst/middle) — together indicating they went into the midst wailing.
segments.s170-g19: همه is the universal quantifier ‘all/everyone’. دیده here is the plural notion ‘eyes’ (noun) and is modified by the adjective پرخون ‘full of blood/bloodshot’. رخساره is ‘face/complexion’ and the following adjective زرد means ‘pale/wan’ (zard here = pale rather than literal yellow). The clause is verbless (stative description) as is common in Persian.
segments.s170-g20: زبان here means ‘tongue’ metonymically = ‘speech’ or ‘every tongue’. از سیاوش is a prepositional phrase ‘concerning/of Siavash’ (از = ‘of/concerning’). پر از is the standard ‘full of’ construction (پر = ‘full (of)’ + از = ‘of’). یادکرد is the noun ‘remembrance’ or ‘mention’ — so the phrase says people’s speech was full of remembrance of Siavash.
segments.s170-g21: چو is the comparative/simile particle ‘like/as’ placed before proper names (poetic usage). The names طوس, گودرز, گیو are listed; دلیر follows گیو as an adjective ‘brave’ (noun + adjective order). The verb is omitted — this is a roster of persons compared or invoked by likeness.
segments.s170-g22: Again چو introduces a list ‘like Shapur and Farhad and Roham’; شیر follows رهام as an epithet ‘the lion’ (metaphorical epithet placed after the name). The clause is nominal and elliptical (no verb), common in poetic lists.
segments.s170-g23: همه quantifies ‘everyone’. جامه کرده is a verbal-adjectival phrase where کرده (past participle of کردن) means ‘having put on’ — so ‘had dressed (in) garments’. کبود و سیاه are the colors of those garments (‘dark blue/azure and black’), adjectives following the noun as usual.
segments.s170-g24: همه again ‘everyone’. خاک بر سر is an idiomatic mourning formula ‘dust/ashes on (their) head’ (خاک here = dust). بر = ‘on/upon’ and سر = ‘head’. بجای کلاه means ‘in place of hat’ or ‘instead of hats’ (بجای = ‘instead of’, کلاه = ‘hat’), indicating they threw dust on their heads where hats would be.
segments.s170-g33: This is a verbless, compact poetic clause where the preposition به introduces the instrument/means ('by/with'). چنگال رخساره is an instrumental phrase 'the claws (or nails) of the face' (i.e., scratching his cheeks), and بشخود is an archaic/poetic reflexive/performative expression attached to the subject زال ('Zāl') — the whole line reads as 'Zāl, by the nails of his face, scratched himself.' Word order is inverted for emphasis (instrument before actor).
segments.s170-g34: همی is the continuous/progressive particle (marks ongoing action). ریخت here is a verbal root meaning 'to pour/fall' (used intransitively), and the prepositional combination از بر = 'from off/of' locates the falling dust with respect to شاخ و یال ('horn and mane'). The clause literally means 'dust was pouring off (from) the horns and mane.'
segments.s170-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; یک هفته is a duration 'for a week' and با سوگ 'in mourning' complements the copula بود (past 'was'). دژم is a predicate adjective 'sullen/grim.' The sequence describes his state during that week.
segments.s170-g36: به هشتم is a temporal adverbial 'on the eighth (day)'; برآمد is an intransitive verb meaning 'arose/issued forth' and is followed by the source phrase ز شیپور دم literally 'the breath/blast from the trumpet.' The verb+source order is typical poetic phrasing: 'on the eighth, a blast issued from the trumpet.'
segments.s170-g37: سپاهی فراوان is a noun phrase 'a plentiful army/a great force.' بر پیلتن is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/to Piltan' (بر here = toward/at). In Persian poetry the place phrase can precede or follow the subject for rhythmical reasons.
segments.s170-g38: ز کشمیر و کابل is a source phrase 'from Kashmir and Kabul.' شدند is the past plural of شدن 'to become/happen' used here with انجمن (assembly/meeting) to mean 'they assembled / became an assembly' — verb before noun is a common poetic inversion meaning 'they formed an assembly.'
segments.s170-g39: به درگاه کاووس is a prepositional phrase 'to the court of Kavus.' بنهاد is an older past-tense verb 'placed/put' (here idiomatically 'showed/presented'), and روی (face) is the direct object: 'he presented his face to the court of Kavus.' The verb+object order is standard but compact in poetry.
segments.s170-g40: دو دیده پر از آب is a noun phrase 'two eyes full of water' (i.e., tear-filled eyes), where پر از is a fixed adjectival construction 'full of.' دل کینه جوی is a compound where جوی (here 'seeking') modifies کینه ('hatred/grudge') to form 'heart seeking vengeance' or 'vengeful heart.' The two phrases are presented in apposition, describing his appearance and inner state.
segments.s170-g121: This is a compact prepositional clause: ز میدان (from the arena) marks source/place, followed by یکی بانگ (a single cry) as the subject, and برشد (past tense, 3rd‑person singular) 'rose' as the verb. The final prepositional phrase به ابر 'to the cloud(s)' marks the direction/target of the rising cry. Word order (source — subject — verb — goal) is typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s170-g122: تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past (you said); it introduces reported content. زمین شد (earth became) uses the past of شدن to indicate a change of state. به کام هژبر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for the desire (or pleasure) of Hojbar' (کام = satisfaction/fulfillment, هژبر = a proper name). The clause reports what was said: that the earth came to Hojbar's satisfaction.
segments.s170-g123: بزد is past 3rd‑person 'he struck/smote' and governs the direct object مهره 'a bead/ornament' (here an object struck). بر پشت پیلان is a locative phrase 'on the backs of the elephants' (بر + پشت + plural پیلان). به جام is a simile or manner phrase 'as/like a cup' (جام functioning adverbially). The clause strings action + object + location + manner in compact poetic order.
segments.s170-g124: This is a straightforward past plural clause: یلان (heroes) is the subject, کشیدند is past 3rd‑person plural of کشیدن 'they drew', used here with the specialized sense 'drew (weapons)'. تیغ is the object 'swords', and از نیام 'from the scabbard' specifies source/origin. Word order Subject — Verb — Object — source is conventional and the preposition از marks extraction from the sheath.
segments.s170-g125: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth' and takes the infinitive/masdar خروشیدن 'roaring' as its subject or verbal noun ('a roaring arose'). گاودم is a nominal image appended to the clause (literally 'cow's tail') — in poetic usage it evokes a loud-blowing instrument or a tail‑like standard; here it functions as part of the enumerated sounds/images tied to the roar. The line places the action (arising) before the concrete images that realize it.
segments.s170-g126: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing sound-producing items: دم نای 'the breath of the reed (flute)' (دم = breath/moment, نای = ney/reed‑flute), و joining رویین و رویینه خم 'the iron/armored pipe and the iron‑bent curve' (رویین/رویینه = iron‑hard/armored, خم = curve/bend). Grammatically these nouns enumerate what contributed to the roar; adjectives (رویین/رویینه) directly modify the following noun خم.
segments.s170-g127: This clause uses the copular/becoming construction: جهان پر شد 'the world became full' (شد = became, پر = full of). از کین افراسیاب is an agentive/causal genitive: 'with/by the hatred of Afrasiab' (از marks source/contents here). The whole phrase expresses that the world was filled with Afrasiab's enmity.
segments.s170-g128: به دریا 'into/at the sea' sets the location, then تو گفتی 'you said' introduces the reported clause. به جوش آمد آب is an intransitive result clause: به جوش آمد 'came to a boil' with آب 'water' as the subject; جوش here is a state noun 'boiling/surge'. The sequence reports the statement that the sea's waters boiled up.
segments.s170-g81: بیامد is a third-person past singular verb 'came' (poetic past form appears up front here). به درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the court/presence' marking destination. با سوگ و درد is an accompanying/adverbial phrase 'with mourning and sorrow' that modifies his manner as he came.
segments.s170-g82: پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر followed by از + noun). خون دل و دیده is a poetic coordinating phrase 'blood of the heart and (of the) eye(s)' (metaphorical/bodily image). رخساره زرد is a noun + adjective sequence 'face (complexion) pale' where the adjective follows the noun, a normal Persian order.
segments.s170-g83: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'the whole/entire city of Iran' (همه modifies شهر). به ماتم شدند uses به + noun to indicate entering a state ('became into mourning'); شدند is past plural 'they became/went'.
segments.s170-g84: پر از درد is again 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر + از + noun). نزدیک رستم شدند is a predication 'they became/ came near Rostam' where نزدیک Rستم is a locative/relational phrase and شدند is the past plural verb showing the action/state applies to the same subject.
segments.s170-g85: چو یک هفته is a temporal clause 'when/after one week' (چو = when). با سوگ و با آب چشم is a coordinated instrumental/attendant phrase 'with mourning and with the water of the eyes' (آبِ چشم = tears); the repeated با is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s170-g86: به درگاه بنشست is the main clause 'he sat down at the court/presence' (بنشست = past 'sat down'). پر درد و خشم is a predicative phrase 'full of pain and anger' placed after the verb; note the poet omits از after پر (a common poetic compression).
segments.s170-g87: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth' (day implied). بزد is the past third-person singular 'he struck' or 'he sounded'. نای رویین و کوس are the direct objects 'the iron ney (reed-flute) and the drum' coordinated with و; رویین qualifies نای as 'iron/metal'.
segments.s170-g88: بیامد به درگاه is another past clause 'he came to the court/presence' (بیامد = 'came'). گودرز و طوس are proper names in the object of the preposition به, here meaning 'to Goudarz and Tous' (طوس is the hero/person, not the city).
segments.s170-g137: ز + noun and از + noun are two parallel prepositional phrases meaning 'from X' (ز is the poetic variant of از). The conjunction و links them. بیشه نارون is a genitive/noun–noun phrase 'grove of Narun' with the ezāfe omitted (common in epic diction), so more literally 'the Narun grove.'
segments.s170-g138: ده و دو هزار is the numeral 'ten and two thousand' = 12,000; Persian often strings number words this way. از یلان انجمن is a compact poetic ordering meaning 'an assembly from (among) the heroes' (انجمن 'assembly' follows یلان without an explicit ezāfe). There is no finite verb here; the clause is nominal and describes the gathered force.
segments.s170-g139: سپه را marks 'the army' with the object/topic marker را in a copular/inverted predication pattern (سپه را X بود → X was of/the army). فرامرز is the predicative noun, and بد پیش‌رو is an adjectival epithet (بد preceding پیش‌رو); in older poetry adjectives can precede their head. پیش‌رو here means 'forerunner/leader' or 'vanguard.'
segments.s170-g140: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause about فرامرز. فرزند گو بود uses گو in the sense of a reporting/formative element ('-said' / 'so-called' or 'it is said'), so the clause means 'who was said to be a son' (i.e., reputedly a son of some lineage). و سالار نو coordinates a second predicate 'and (was) a new commander' with نو as an attributive adjective 'new' (recently appointed). بود is the past copula.
segments.s170-g141: همی رفت is an iterative/continuous past construction ('kept going' or 'was going'); همی is an archaic/poetic aspectual particle. تا introduces the endpoint 'until' and precedes مرز توران ('the border of Turan'), and رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s170-g142: ز دشمن modifies کسی (literally 'of/from the enemy'), کسی را is the object marked with را, and the phrase به ره بر ندید is an idiomatic periphrasis meaning 'did not meet on the way' (بر as part of the verbal idiom + دید 'see/meet'); ندید negates the action. In context کسی را ... ندید means 'he saw/met no one.'
segments.s170-g143: دران is an archaic locative ('in that'), placing the scene at that border (مرز). شاه سپیجاب is the nominative phrase 'King Sepyab' (title + name; ezāfe is omitted in epic style), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The line therefore states existence/location: 'In that frontier was King Sepyab.'
segments.s170-g144: که introduces a relative clause describing the king. با before each noun is a simple possessive/associative 'with' (he had/was accompanied by) listing لشکر (army), گنج (treasure), and آب (water); the repeated و simply links the items. بود at the end is the copula tying these possessions to the king ('who had ...').
segments.s170-g337: چنین گفت رستم = “Rostam said thus.” چَنین ('thus/so') is an adverb placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here reported speech marker) and رستم is the subject following the verb, a common word order in older Persian (V‑S‑O). که‌گر is an archaic fused conditional ('that if' / 'if'), introducing the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s170-g338: چنان خسته‌دل شاید و سوگوار = the conditional clause: 'if the king is so heavy‑hearted and mourning.' چنان ('so/thus') modifies خسته‌دل; خسته‌دل is an adjectival compound meaning 'weary‑hearted.' شاید here is an epistemic/optative particle meaning 'may be' or 'it may be/ perhaps' (used to express possibility), and و links سوگوار ('mourning') as a second predicate adjective describing the same subject (شهریار implied from the previous clause).
segments.s170-g339: همیشه دل و جان افراسیاب = 'May Afrasiab's heart and life always…' همیشه ('always') is an adverb that scopes over the whole wish; دل و جان are a paired noun phrase 'heart and life/soul' and افراسیاب is the proper name in the genitive/possessive relationship (the possessor follows the possessed in older Persian style). The whole phrase serves as the subject of the optative that follows.
segments.s170-g340: پر از درد باد و دو دیده پرآب = 'be full of pain, and (have) two eyes full of water.' پر از 'full of' precedes the noun of content (درد). باد here is the classical optative particle ('may it be'), so the clause is a wish. و links the second coordinated clause 'دو دیده پرآب' where دو دیده = 'two eyes' and پرآب = 'full of water/tears.' Word order is adjective/phrase after the noun, a normal pattern for predicative complements in classical Persian.
segments.s170-g341: همان تشت و خنجر زواره ببرد = 'He took that basin and dagger to Zowareh.' همان ('that/the same') points back to previously mentioned items; تشت و خنجر is a coordinated direct object. زواره is a proper name (place or person) indicating destination or recipient; ببرد is a past or narrative preterite meaning 'he carried/took.' The object precedes the verb, consistent with older Persian narrative order.
segments.s170-g342: بدان روزبانان لشکر سپرد = 'he entrusted them to those heralds of the army.' بدَان is the classical compound (ب = 'to/unto' + دان = 'those/that') functioning as 'to those'; روزبانان لشکر is the dative/indirect object 'the army’s heralds' (روزبانان = heralds, لشکر qualifies them), and سپرد is the verb 'he entrusted/handed over.' The verb comes at the end, completing the action begun in the previous segment.
segments.s170-g343: سرش را به خنجر ببرید زار = 'They cut off his head with the dagger, wailing.' سرش (سر + ش = 'his head') plus the object marker را marks the definite object. به خنجر indicates instrument ('with the dagger'). ببرید here is the narrative verb meaning 'cut off' (contextual reading; the supplied lemma list is inconsistent), and زار follows as an adverb/adjectival predicate meaning 'lamenting/woeful' modifying the action. The sequence places object before verb, then an adverbial comment.
segments.s170-g344: زمانی خروشید و برگشت کار = 'For a time he cried out, and the affair reversed.' زمانی ('for a time/for a while') is an adverbial phrase; خروشید is an intransitive past verb 'he roared/cried out.' و links to برگشت کار where برگشت is a noun/verb indicating 'return' or 'reversal' and کار 'the matter/affair' names what was reversed. The clause states a short sequence of reactions and the reversal of the situation.
segments.s170-g329: مرا here is the older possessive/object form meaning 'my' (literally 'to me') used with a body-part noun; دیده پرآب is a predicative nominal phrase 'eye full-of-water' = 'tear-filled eye'. بد روز و شب follows as an adverbial phrase 'bad day and night' (poetic inversion) indicating the continual unhappy state. Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: possessor + body part + adjective phrase + temporal phrase.
segments.s170-g330: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. به نفرین is a prepositional phrase here meaning 'with/in a curse' (marks the content or manner). گشاده is an adjective 'open' modifying لب (lips); the phrase گشاده دو لب literally 'the two lips are open' — in Persian the adjective follows the noun phrase it qualifies. The clause gives the habitual state: 'my two lips are always open in curse.'
segments.s170-g331: بران کس is an emphatic classical construction 'that very man / let that man' (بران here functions like 'in that case' or 'let him be'). که introduces a relative clause 'who...'; آن تشت و خنجر گرفت is a simple past clause 'took that basin and dagger' with گرفت as the past-tense verb 'seized/took'. Word order: demonstrative/emphatic + noun + relative clause + verb.
segments.s170-g332: The pattern repeats: بران کس 'that/let that person' + که (relative) + آن شاه را (direct object marked by را) + سرگرفت. سرگرفت is a past-form verb; here it means 'seized/grasped (the head/king)' — the را marks شاه as the object. The clause is another relative clause specifying the person by his deed.
segments.s170-g333: دل طوس is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the heart of Tūs' (proper name follows the possessed noun). بخشایش is the direct object 'forgiveness'; آورد is the simple past 'brought' (i.e. 'caused/produced'); سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'strongly/very much' modifying آورد. Overall: 'Tūs's heart strongly brought (forth) forgiveness.'
segments.s170-g334: بران here means 'for that' or 'on that account' linking to the previous clause; نامبردار is 'the aforesaid/afore-named (person)' (classical poetic adjective); برگشته modifies بخت and means 'turned/back-turned' (i.e. reversed); بخت is 'fortune/fate'. The phrase describes the person as the afore-named one whose fortune has turned (ill).
segments.s170-g335: بر رستم آمد is a common classical phrasing meaning 'he came to / approached Rostam' (بر + person = toward). آمد is past 'came', and بگفت is the past narrative form 'he said'. این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this speech' placed after the verb cluster, typical in epic narration.
segments.s170-g336: که introduces reported content ('that...'). پور سپهدار is a compound: پور 'son (of)' + سپهدار 'the commander (a title)' = 'the commander's son'. افگند is simple past 'threw/cast (down)'; بن is a noun 'root/foundation' used here as the object of افگند. The clause reports what was said about that man's action.
segments.s170-g217: چو/که آمد به + place + for-purpose: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'when/that' here. آمد is simple past 'came'. به marks motion toward the place name پیلتن. کین ('vengeance') follows به to indicate purpose ('to take vengeance'); رستم is the subject appearing after the purpose phrase (typical Persian subject placement after adverbials in poetry).
segments.s170-g218: بزرگان ایران شدند انجمن is a nominal clause where بزرگان ایران ('the great men of Iran') is the subject and شدند ('became') is the past-tense copula meaning 'assembled/formed'. انجمن (assembly) is the predicate noun; the verb indicates formation or coming together rather than a progressive action.
segments.s170-g219: ورازاد را سر بریدند is a straight transitive clause: ورازاد (object) is marked by را, سر ('head') is the direct object qualified by the verb بریدند (past 'they cut'). زار immediately following functions as a noun 'a wail/cry' (poetic placement) that links to the next verb as its subject.
segments.s170-g220: زار برانگیخت is an intransitive sequence 'a wail arose' where برانگیخت is past tense 'arose/was roused'. از مرز توران دمار is a prepositional phrase 'from the border of Turan' (از = from), with دمار ('ruin/calamity') as the noun affected; the whole gives the consequence or wider effect of the killing.
segments.s170-g221: سپه را سراسر بهم بر زدند: سپه را (the army, marked by را) is the object; سراسر 'throughout/utterly' is an adverbial intensifier; بهم here indicates 'into confusion' or 'together/in disorder'; بر زدند (literally 'struck upon') idiomatically means they routed or smashed it. The verb is past plural 'they struck/overturned.'
segments.s170-g222: به بوم و به بر آتش اندر زدند is a compound locative + verb: به بوم و به بر 'to dwelling/homestead and to the field' (repetition of به for emphasis), آتش اندر زدند means 'they put/struck fire within' — poetically 'they set fire to house and land.' اندر is an older poetic preposition 'in/within'; زدند is past plural 'they struck/placed.'
segments.s170-g223: چو بشنید افراسیاب این سخن: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; بشنید is past 'he heard'; افراسیاب is the subject (name) that follows the verb — a common epic inversion; این سخن 'these words/this speech' is the object of hearing.
segments.s170-g224: غمی شد ز کردارهای کهن: غمی (grief) is the subject noun and شد is past 'became' — 'grief arose' or 'he became sorrowful.' ز (poetic form of از) introduces the cause 'because of/from' and کردارهای کهن is the genitive phrase 'the ancient/past deeds' giving the reason for the grief.
segments.s170-g89: چو here is the comparative particle 'as/like' placed before a coordinated list; Persian allows repeated چو to set up parallel groups. The names are simply coordinated with و 'and' between them; the sequence is nominative/name list functioning as the comparison's target.
segments.s170-g90: This segment repeats چو 'as/like' to introduce a second parallel list. شاپور and the following نیو form a name + epithet/unit; in poetry epithets or regional forms can follow names. The final word نیو appears as an epithet/qualifier attached to شاپور rather than a separate verb or noun.
segments.s170-g91: Two personal names (فریبرز، کاووس) are followed by an epithet phrase درنده شیر ('ferocious lion'). The adjective درنده precedes شیر (adjective + noun), a common poetic order; the whole phrase is appositional, attributing the quality of a 'ferocious lion' to the named figures.
segments.s170-g92: گرازه is the head of a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that'; بود is the past copula 'was'; اژدهای دلیر is a predicate nominative with the noun 'dragon' modified by the adjective 'دلیر' (brave/valiant). The relative clause describes گرازه as being a valiant dragon.
segments.s170-g93: Two names (فرامرز رستم) are followed by a relative clause beginning with که 'who'. بد پیش رو is an idiomatic, compact phrase: بد functions as an emphatic/pejorative adjective with a sense of 'formidable' or 'fearsome', and پیش رو (literally 'front-face') means 'in front' or 'at the fore'. The sequence places the descriptive phrase after the names, typical in epic verse.
segments.s170-g94: نگهبان 'guardian' is a predicate noun describing role; هر 'every' quantifies مرز 'border/frontier'. و links to سالار 'commander/lord', and نو is an adjective 'new' modifying سالار. The noun + modifiers are in the normal Persian order (determiner/adjective after the noun is possible in poetry), giving two coordinated titles: guardian of every border and a new commander.
segments.s170-g95: به گردان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the host/troop' (به + گردان). چنین گفت رستم is the poetic word order where the verb گفت 'said' and manner adverb چنین 'thus' come before the subject رستم. که introduces the subordinate clause that contains Rostam's speech; من 'I' begins that clause.
segments.s170-g96: برین is a compound of بر 'on/against/to' + این (orthographic ین) meaning 'this' — here 'to/for this'. کینه 'hatred/grudge' is the object; دادم is the first-person past 'I gave' used idiomatically as 'I have devoted/pledged'. دل و جان و تن is a triad of direct objects ('heart, life, and body') linked by و 'and'.
segments.s170-g113: وگر نه is a conditional phrase ('but if not' / 'if not'); here it introduces an alternative. The sequence من و گرز و شمشیر تیز lists the speaker together with his weapons ('I and (my) mace and sharp sword'), where و is the simple coordinating 'and' and تیز is a postpositive adjective modifying شمشیر ('sharp sword'). The whole phrase sets up the agent/companions for the following verb.
segments.s170-g114: برانگیزم is first-person singular (optative/intentional) of برانگیختن, meaning 'I will rouse' / 'may I rouse' — here a resolved future-like act. اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/throughout the world'. رستخیز is the nominal object 'resurrection' used figuratively as 'uprising' or mass stirring; it is what will be roused.
segments.s170-g115: نبیند is a third-person negated verb form 'do(es) not see' (classical order places the verb first). دو چشمم ('my two eyes') is the possessed noun phrase (م is the enclitic for 'my') that is the grammatical subject/possessor. مگر here means 'except', and گرد رزم is a genitive phrase 'the dust of battle' (گرد = 'dust', رزم = 'battle') — so the clause states that nothing but battlefield dust is seen.
segments.s170-g116: حرامست is an archaic contraction of حرام است, meaning 'is forbidden'; it functions as the predicate. بر من marks the person affected ('to me' / 'for me'). می‌و جام و بزم lists the forbidden things: می (wine), جام (cup), and بزم (banquet/revelry), joined by و. The clause therefore states that wine, the cup, and feasting are forbidden to the speaker.
segments.s170-g117: به درگاه is a locative phrase 'at/to the court/threshold/presence of'. هر پهلوانی ('every champion/hero') is the quantified noun (the -ی marks the indefinite/relative), and که بود is a relative clause 'who was (there)' — که introduces the relative and بود is past/indicative 'was'. The whole phrase sets the scene: 'at the court of every champion present'.
segments.s170-g118: چو = 'when'. زان گونه = 'of that kind / in that manner' (زان = 'that', گونه = 'manner/kind'). آواز رستم is a possessive (the voice/cry of Rostam). شنود is third-person singular present/habitual 'he hears' or simple pastive hearing in classical style; it links back to هر پهلوانی so that the sense is 'when each champion heard Rostam's voice of that kind.'
segments.s170-g119: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. برگرفتند is past-tense plural 'they lifted / they uttered / they took up' (here 'they raised' a cry). با او 'with him' marks accompaniment. خروش ('a shout/roar') is the object — the clause means 'they all raised a shout along with him.'
segments.s170-g120: تو گفتی is 'you said' (تو = you, گفتی = 2nd person past 'said'); که introduces reported content ('that'). میدان برآمد uses میدان 'field/arena' as subject and برآمد (past 'arose/issued forth') as the verb. به جوش is a prepositional/metaphorical phrase 'into boiling / into a surge' (جوش = boiling/surge), describing the battlefield's agitation. Together: 'you said that the field rose in tumult.'
segments.s170-g169: به کین is an adverbial prepositional phrase of purpose/causation ("for vengeance"), placed at the sentence start for emphasis. سیاوش is the subject (proper name). کمر بر میان is a noun phrase ("a belt/about the waist, at the middle") which in Persian can appear before the verb; the verb that governs it (to gird/tie) appears in the next segment.
segments.s170-g170: ببست is the past tense (3rd person) of بستن (here "he girded/tied"); in older/poetic Persian the prosthetic b- appears (ببست) instead of the colloquial بست. و simply links the two actions. بیامد is past tense "he came forth" (بی- is an older prefix found in classical texts). چو is the comparative/simile marker "like/as," and شیر ژیان is a poetic simile "a lion (ژیان used attributively)" meaning a fierce/raging lion.
segments.s170-g171: برآرد is a classical present/past form meaning "he brings forth/raises" (here past/epic narrative sense). از این مرز (written ازین مرز in the line) is a prepositional phrase "from this/that border/limit." بی‌ارز modifies مرز to mean "worthless/ignoble border," and دود is the direct object "smoke/fume." Word order places the verb before or after phrase elements freely in poetry.
segments.s170-g172: هوا گرد is a noun + postpositive modifier meaning "the air around/about (him)." او را is the focused object ("to him" with the object-marker را), and نیارد here is an archaic/poetic negative form equivalent to نیاورد ("does not bring"); the clause ends with بسود "to advantage/for benefit." The whole means that the surrounding air brought him no advantage. (Note: the provided lemma senses for نیارد did not match this verbal sense.)
segments.s170-g173: ورازاد is the subject (here a proper name or epithet). بشنید is the past tense verb "he heard." گفتار او is the object noun phrase "his speech/words." The order is straightforward subject–verb–object; classical Persian often places such elements flexibly for meter.
segments.s170-g174: همی is an adverbial particle giving immediacy or emphasis (here something like "straightaway/at once"). خوار is a predicative adjective "despicable/worthless," and دانست is the past tense of دانستن used in the sense "deemed/considered." پیگار او ("his battle/cause") is the object of that deeming. So the construction literally says "he considered his cause despicable."
segments.s170-g175: به لشکر is the indirect object/recipient phrase "to the army" (i.e., he addressed the army). بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb "he commanded/said." کاندر is an adverb meaning "therein/into that (place)," and دهید is the present/imperfect/imperative form (here a command directed to the army) "give/put/set." Together this reports he told the army to put (something) there.
segments.s170-g176: کمان‌ها is the plural object "the bows." سراسر is an adverb "entirely/throughout/all of them." به زه literally "with/onto the bowstring" (به here marks the instrument/target of the action), and بر نهید is the verb phrase (نهید = نهاد, "place/set/put on") in the jussive/imperative sense addressed to the army (2nd person plural). The whole is an imperative: "String all the bows with the bowstring."
segments.s170-g129: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past of بودن meaning "there was not." The phrase جای پوینده را is the thing whose existence is denied (را marks the definite object/emphasized noun), and بر زمین gives the location "on the ground." Word order is verb (negative existential) + object phrase + locative.
segments.s170-g130: ز introduces the source/cause here ("from/through").‌ نیزه هوا ماند literalizes as "the spear remained in the air" (هوا = "air/in mid‑air"); ماند is simple past "remained/was left." اندر کمین is a locative adjunct "in ambush/lying in wait," modifying the state of being poised.
segments.s170-g131: ستاره is the subject "the star." The sequence به جنگ اندر آمد is a poetic way to say "entered into battle" (به + noun + اندر آمد = "came into/entered"). نخست is an adverb "first/at first," placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s170-g132: زمین و زمان is a compound subject ("earth and time"). دست خون را is the definite object phrase (دست modified by خون = "hand stained/by blood"; را marks it as the object), and بشست is the past tense verb "washed." Literally: "earth and time washed the (blood‑stained) hand," idiomatically: they "washed their hands in blood" (i.e. widespread bloodshed).
segments.s170-g133: ببستند is 3rd person plural past "they closed/they drew up/form(ed)." گردان ایران میان is a noun phrase meaning "the Iranian troops in the middle/center" (گردان ایران = "the Iranian host/troop," میان = locative "in the middle"). The verb precedes the noun phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s170-g134: به پیش functions as "before/in the presence of." اندرون اختر کاویان is a genitive/attributive string: اندرون = "inner/within," اختر = "star" (here a title/epithet), کاویان = "the Kayanian(s)." Together it means "before the inner presence/inner court of the Kayanian star/king." The whole segment gives the place where the troops were positioned.
segments.s170-g135: گزین کرد is the compound past tense verb (گزین + کردن) meaning "chose/selected." پس is an adverb "then/afterward." The subject follows the verb in poetic order: رستم زابلی = "Rostam the Zabulite."
segments.s170-g136: ز marks origin "from/of." گردان here means "troop/ranks," and شمشیرزن کابلی is a noun with attributive adjective (شمشیرزن = "swordsman," کابلی = "Kabulite/of Kabul"). The segment reads as "from the troop, (he chose) a Kabul swordsman" (the phrase may also mean "from the troop of Kabul swordsmen").
segments.s170-g49: چو آمد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' + آمد (past tense 'came'). به نزدیک + کاووس is a prepositional phrase 'near Kavus' (به = toward/into; نزدیک = near). کی here functions as a relative/identifying pronoun ('who' / 'whose') linking into the next clause.
segments.s170-g50: سرش is سر + possessive suffix ش ('his/her head'); بود is the past copula 'was'. پرخاک is an adjectival predicate 'full of dust/dusty'; the repetition پرخاک و پرخاک is a poetic emphatic doubling. پی here means 'after/behind' (i.e. dusty behind/at the rear).
segments.s170-g51: بدو گفت is 'said to him': بدو = 'to him/unto him' (prepositional phrase) + گفت (past 'said'). خوی بد is the noun phrase 'ill-disposed / bad-natured', and ای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' addressed to Kavus (ای as the vocative marker).
segments.s170-g52: This is a condensed, poetic clause: پراگندی و تخمت are abstract nouns (a sense of 'defilement/reproach' and a related insult), followed by آمد ببار which together describe a coming down or pouring ('came' + 'poured'). آمد is the past verb 'came' and ببار (literally 'pour/rain') completes the verbal image—'came pouring down.'
segments.s170-g53: ترا is the object form 'to you / you (accusative)' (here written as ت + را), placed before the following nouns for emphasis. مهر سودابه = 'the love/affection of Sudabeh' and بدخوی = 'ill-tempered / bad disposition' — two noun phrases coordinated by و. Word order is emphatic/poetic (object fronted).
segments.s170-g54: ز = 'from' (poetic), سر = 'head', برگرفت is the past transitive verb 'took up / took away'. افسر خسروی 'the kingly/royal helmet' is the agent; the line uses a somewhat inverted, poetic word order: 'from the head (برگرفت) the royal helmet'.
segments.s170-g55: کنون = 'now'; آشکارا is an adverb 'clearly / openly'. ببینی is the second-person present/future form 'you see' (in Ferdowsi often rendered as 'you will see' or 'you clearly see'), and همی is an archaic continuative/emphatic particle reinforcing the present/future aspect.
segments.s170-g56: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; بر موج دریا is a prepositional phrase 'on the wave(s) of the sea' (بر = on, موج = wave, دریا = sea). نشینی is the second-person present 'you sit' (the subject 'تو' is omitted), and the final همی again marks the ongoing/iterative aspect.
segments.s170-g297: سپاه is the subject ('the army/host'). آفرین خواند is a past action: خواند (past of زدن/خواندن here) means 'uttered/sang/called' and آفرین is the thing uttered ('an acclamation/bravo'). بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase marking the target ('to/upon the hero'). Word order is topical: subject first, verb + object, then the phrase marking the recipient.
segments.s170-g298: بران is a demonstrative/adnominal pointer ('that/aforementioned') referring back to someone just named. نامبردار modifies پور (an adjective after the noun, as is common in poetic order) giving 'renowned'. پور جوان is a noun + modifier: پور 'son' with جوان 'young' describing him.
segments.s170-g299: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmātan). آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction where آفرین is the object ('a shout of praise') and کرد (past of کردن) is the verb 'made/did' → 'praised'. نیز 'also' attaches to the clause. The item برو here does not mean 'go' in the sense listed; it functions as a deictic/locative/clitic in older poetic usage (see note) and is not the literal 'go' from the provided senses.
segments.s170-g300: به marks the indirect object 'to/for the dervish'. بخشید is past of بخشیدن here meaning 'bestowed/gave'. بسیار چیز is a quantified noun phrase: بسیار 'many' modifying چیز 'things'. Word order is typical: indirect object (to dervish) + verb + quantity + noun.
segments.s170-g301: یکی here is indefinite 'a certain' or 'someone'. داستان زد uses زد (past of زدن) in the sense 'to sound/perform/recite' (i.e., 'told/sang a tale'). برو پیلتن places the action at or toward the place Pilten; as above, برو here is a classical/poetic locative/deictic and not the simple 'go' in the modern sense.
segments.s170-g302: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/for that'. هر کس که is the standard 'whoever/whichever person'. سر برکشد is a present/subjunctive-like construction with برکشد (بر + کشیدن) meaning 'lifts/raises (his) head' — here used as a general condition. ز انجمن is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/out of the assembly' (ز = from).
segments.s170-g303: خرد باید is a modal/predicative phrase: خرد ('wisdom') with باید ('must/it is necessary') giving the sense 'one must have wisdom' or 'wisdom is required'. و links the next noun. گوهر نامدار is a noun phrase 'a renowned/famed gem' with the adjective following the noun (common in Persian poetry).
segments.s170-g304: This is a pair of appositive/predicative noun phrases: هنر یار = 'skill/art (be) companion' (یار = companion) and فرهنگش آموزگار = 'his culture (be) teacher', where -ش on فرهنگ is the 3rd-person possessive 'his'. The copula 'is' is omitted as normal in Persian, giving imperative/recommendatory force to the qualities prescribed.
segments.s170-g345: بریده is a past participle/adjective 'severed' placed before the nouns it modifies (typical poetic order). سر و تنش = 'his head and body' where ش is the possessive enclitic 'his'. بر دار is a locative compound 'on the gallows' and کرد is the simple past 'did/placed' (third person), so the clause means 'they put his severed head and body on the gallows.'
segments.s170-g346: دو پایش = 'his two feet' (دو numeral + noun + possessive ش). زبر سر is an archaic compound meaning 'above/over the head' (زبر = 'above'); نگونسار is an adjectival/resultative word describing the posture (here rendered 'upside‑down' / 'face‑down' in context) and کرد (past) makes the predicate ('they made him نگونسار' = 'they hung him that way').
segments.s170-g347: بران is an older adverb/prepositional form meaning 'upon/thereon' and combines with کشته 'the slain' (here a noun). از کین is an adverbial phrase 'out of/from vengeance/hatred', and برافشاند is a perfective verb 'scattered' — so the clause reads 'they scattered dust upon the slain out of hatred.'
segments.s170-g348: تنش را is the object 'his body' with the definite/object marker را and possessive ش. به خنجر is an instrumental phrase 'with (a) dagger' (به used instrumentally here). بکردند is past tense 'they made/did' (3rd plural) and چاک is a noun 'gash/tear' used resultatively after the verb: 'they made gashes in his body with daggers.'
segments.s170-g349: جهانا is a vocative 'O world' (–a vocative ending). چه خواهی is a rhetorical present/future 'what do you want/what will you ask', where خواهی is 2nd singular. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and پروردگان here means 'the rulers/lords' — so 'O world, what do you want from the rulers?'
segments.s170-g350: چه at the start is an exclamatory/rhetorical 'what' (here 'what [are] the rulers...'). پروردگان repeats 'rulers/lords'. داغ دل is a compound 'the burning grief/sore of the heart' and بردگان (here) means 'the servants/slaves/subjects' — together 'the rulers are the burning sorrow in the hearts of the servants.' The line uses elliptic, poetic word order rather than an explicit copula.
segments.s170-g193: فرامرز جنگی is a nominal phrase: proper name + epithet ('Framarz the warrior'). چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. او is the object 'him' marked by the object marker را, and بدید is the past tense verb 'saw' placed at the end of the clause (typical Persian SOV/order in classical lines).
segments.s170-g194: خروشی is the thing raised ('a cry/roar'). چو is a simile marker 'like', شیر is 'lion', ژیان is an archaic adjective/noun modifying شیر ('of the living/valiant' in poetic use), and برکشید is simple past 'he raised/uttered' (here 'raised a roar'). Word order places the object phrase before the verb.
segments.s170-g195: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'roused/raised' (transitive here); از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (its) place'; شبرنگ is the direct object (marked after it by را) — a poetic/technical word (here a named item or piece of tack, 'shabrang'). The clause is compact: 'he lifted X from its place.'
segments.s170-g196: بیفشرد is a past action 'he pressed/pushed' (poetic usage here). بر + نیزه 'on/upon the spear' gives the place of action, and بر + چنگ را 'against the claw' (چنگ = claw/talon) with را marking the object affected. The prepositional بر repeats to indicate successive contacts ('onto the spear, onto the claw').
segments.s170-g197: یکی functions as an indefinite subject 'one/someone'. نیزه is the instrument 'spear' and زد is the past verb 'struck/thrust'. The target is expressed by بر + کمربند او 'against his belt' (no را here — acceptable when the object is governed by a preposition).
segments.s170-g198: که introduces the resultant clause 'so that/and then'. بگسست is past passive/ intransitive 'it broke/was torn apart'. زیر زره بند او is a genitive/locative sequence 'the band/strap under his armor' (زیر = under; بند = band/strap here), with او marking possession.
segments.s170-g199: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' intensifying the action. برگرفتش is برگرفت + enclitic object -ش 'he took/raised him' (the -ش = 'him'); ز (poetic form of از) + زین 'from the saddle' and خدنگ 'dagger' specify the source: 'he took him from the saddle's dagger.'
segments.s170-g200: که again introduces a comparative gloss 'as (people) say'. گفتی is a parenthetical 'one says/you might say' (poetic usage). یک پشه دارد به چنگ is a simile: 'a (single) fly is in the claw' — یک پشه is subject, دارد is present-tense 'has', and به چنگ is the locative/complement 'in its claw'. The whole gives the image of how lightly he was lifted.
segments.s170-g289: This is a noun phrase giving the thing and time: درفش تهمتن = 'Tahmatan's standard' (darfsh + epithet), followed by the temporal/locative phrase همانگه ز راه = 'at that moment/from the road'. Word order is noun + epithet + time phrase; ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'.
segments.s170-g290: پدید آمد is a simple past verb phrase 'appeared/was made manifest' (پدید + آمد). The conjunction و links a following noun phrase: گرد پیل و سپاه — here گرد functions as a noun 'throng/host' and is coordinated with پیل و سپاه 'elephants and army'. In poetic word order the verb may come before or after its related noun phrases; the verb governs the whole clause in the past tense.
segments.s170-g291: فرامرز is the proper name (subject). پیش پدر شد is a short verb phrase where پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' and شد (past of شدن) indicates movement or coming to be (here 'stepped/appeared'). The sequence is name + prepositional phrase + past verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s170-g292: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The clause 'گرد به پیروزی از روزگار نبرد' describes the circumstance: گرد = 'host/throng', به پیروزی = 'with / into victory' (به marking the state/result), از روزگار نبرد = 'from the time/strain of battle' (از + روزگار + نبرد). The whole is a temporally subordinate nominal clause modifying شد.
segments.s170-g293: به پیش اندرون is a directional phrase 'forward / into the interior' (به + پیش + اندرون). سرخه را marks a definite object with را ('the red one' — here a named/referential item, likely a horse), and بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound/tied'; دست follows as the instrument or accompaniment 'by (his) hand'. The clause thus describes motion plus the state of that object.
segments.s170-g294: بکرده is a perfect/participial form 'having done' or 'having made/tied'. ورازاد را is another definite object (ورازاد as an object, here 'Varāzād' or 'the young/offspring'), with را marking it. یال پست is a noun + adjective 'low mane' describing what was bound/handled. The participle bəkarde links these actions as accomplished prior to or accompanying the motion.
segments.s170-g295: همه غار و هامون = 'all caves and plains' (listing places). پر از کشته بود is a stative construction: پر 'full' + از 'of' + کشته 'the slain' + بود (past of بودن) meaning 'were full of slain'. The adjective-like پر precedes the source phrase with از; بود is the past copula.
segments.s170-g296: This is a short past clause: سر دشمن 'the enemy (as head/chief/force)' + از رزم 'from the battle' + برگشته بود 'had turned back/been driven back' (برگشته past participle + بود). برگشته بود expresses past perfective/resultant state: the enemy had been routed/retreated.
segments.s170-g209: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one' placed before the object. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بنوشت is an archaic past-tense verb (equivalent to نوشت) meaning 'wrote' and takes نزد پدر ('to/into the presence of the father') as a prepositional phrase indicating the recipient; نزد here functions like 'to'/'in the presence of'. Word order is topical subject — object — verb with the recipient phrase after the verb.
segments.s170-g210: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/about' introducing the reason or subject of the complaint. کار here means 'deed/affair' (the matter at issue). ورازاد is used as a personal name/epithet (not its literal sense 'offspring' here). پرخاشخر is an adjective/noun 'quarrelsome, aggressive one' modifying or characterizing ورازاد. Together the phrase means 'about Varāzad's quarrelsome deed/behavior.'
segments.s170-g211: که چون is a temporal/relative combination 'that when...' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. برگشادم is an archaic past first-person verb (literally 'I opened' from برگشودن) used here idiomatically as 'I set out / made ready'. در + nouns (کین و جنگ) means 'into / in vengeance and battle' — در governs the two nouns as the sphere or purpose of the action.
segments.s170-g212: ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'him/it' placed before the verb (common in classical Persian). برگرفتم is the past first-person 'I took up / I seized'. ز is the preposition 'from', and زین پلنگ is a genitival phrase 'the saddle of the leopard/panther' (زین = saddle, پلنگ = leopard), so the whole phrase means 'I seized him from the leopard's saddle.'
segments.s170-g213: به کین is a prepositional phrase 'in/for vengeance' and here governs the beneficiary/ground (سیاوش) — 'in vengeance for Siavash.' بریدم is the past first-person 'I cut (off)'. سرش is the direct object 'his head' with the enclitic third-person suffix -ش 'his', so 'I cut off his head.'
segments.s170-g214: برافروختم is a past first-person verb 'I kindled / I set alight'. آتش is the object 'fire'. از کشورش ('from/of his country') specifies the affected place — idiomatically 'I set his country on fire' (از here marks the domain from which the fire spreads). The enclitic ش on کشور marks 'his country.'
segments.s170-g215: وزان سو is a locative expression 'from that side' (وزان = 'and/from that', سو = 'side'). نوندی is here a proper name (a rare name/term) serving as the subject. بیامد is the past third-person 'he came'. به راه ('on the road' or 'along the way') is a prepositional phrase showing the manner or route of his coming.
segments.s170-g216: به نزدیک is a directional phrase 'toward/near'. سالار توران سپاه is a compound nominal phrase: سالار 'commander' qualified by توران 'of Turan', with سپاه 'the army' naming the force; together the phrase means 'toward the commander of Turan's army.' Word order places the directional phrase before the noun cluster it approaches.
segments.s170-g257: A compact noun phrase: خروش (a loud sound/uproar) is the head noun, followed by the genitive/attributive سواران (the riders) and then و + گرد سپاه, where گرد here means the dust or throng raised by the host. The sequence names two simultaneous phenomena (the riders' clamor and the host's dust/array).
segments.s170-g258: A subordinate temporal clause plus main predication compressed in poetry: چو شب کرد = 'when night came/when it became night', where چو functions as 'when' and کرد is a poetic causative/auxiliary meaning 'made/caused (it to be) night' (here rendered simply as 'fell/arrived'). گیتی نهان گشت is a short clause 'the world became hidden' (نهان before the verb, گشت as 'became'); ماه at the end names the celestial object affected (the moon was hidden/veiled).
segments.s170-g259: درخشیدن is a verbal noun (the act of shining) used as the head of the phrase. تیغ (sword/blade) is modified by الماس گون, a compound adjective meaning 'diamond-like' (الماس + گون, the latter marking likeness). Word order is noun + modifiers, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s170-g260: سنانهای is the plural attributive 'spears'; آهار داده is a past-participial phrase 'given/treated with آهار' functioning as a modifier (آهار دادهِ سنانها = 'spears that have been �-treated'). به خون is a prepositional phrase 'with blood' specifying the material/agent of that treatment. Together this describes spears stained/imbued with blood. (In classical diction آهار can mean a finish/preparation, so آهار داده is a participle forming an adjectival phrase.)
segments.s170-g261: تو گفتی is a past-tense 2nd-person report 'you said'. که introduces the content clause; برشد is a (poetically shaped) verb meaning 'rose' or 'came up' (here describing بخار 'vapor' rising) and به گیتی 'into/through the world' is the locative complement. The clause is reported speech compressed into one line.
segments.s170-g262: A simple past verbal clause: برافروختند is 3rd-person plural past 'they kindled' (causative/perfective), with آتش کارزار as the direct object, a genitive compound 'fire of battle' meaning the outbreak or blaze of combat. The verb precedes its object here in normal Persian order for emphasis.
segments.s170-g263: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of'. کشته is used as a substantive 'the slain', modified by the participle فگنده ('thrown'/'scattered'). به هر سو is the adverbial 'to every side', and سران (here 'heads') names what was thrown. The phrase thus reads 'heads of the slain thrown to every side.'
segments.s170-g264: زمین is subject 'the earth/ground'. کوه گشت is a copular phrase: کوه (mountain) + گشت (became) = 'became a mountain'. از کران تا کران is an adverbial range 'from edge to edge' (از ... تا ... = 'from ... to ...'), so the whole clause means the ground turned into a mountain across its whole extent.
segments.s170-g273: ز here is the archaic/pre-classical preposition meaning 'from' (used instead of modern از). The phrase ز ترکان is a prepositional agent/source 'from the Turks' and is followed by the verbal clause به یاری او آمدند, where آمدند is a past plural verb 'they came' placed at the end (classical Persian word order). به یاری ('to help / to his aid') uses به + noun to mark direction/purpose; او is the third‑person pronoun 'him'.
segments.s170-g274: پر از ... آمدند is an adjectival/ adverbial phrase 'they came full of ...' where پر از (literally 'full of') modifies the coming. پر (here) = 'full (of)' and از is the part of the fixed combination 'پر از' meaning 'of'. پرخاشجو is an adjective 'belligerent/aggressive'. The verb آمدند repeats the past plural 'they came' for emphasis, a common poetic device.
segments.s170-g275: This is a pair of causal/prepositional phrases introduced by از ('from/because of'): از آشوب ترکان = 'from the Turks' turmoil' (آشوب + genitive ترکان) and و از رزم سخت = 'and from the hard/fierce battle'. Note the noun + modifier order (رزم سخت = 'fierce battle') and the use of از to mark cause.
segments.s170-g276: فرامرز را marks Faramarz as the definite patient (را = object marker). نیزه شد is a classical passive construction where شد functions as the auxiliary marking 'was [speared / struck by a spear]'. لخت لخت is a reduplicated adjective used resultatively/intensively — here describing his state as torn/stripped (poetic usage); repetition intensifies the effect.
segments.s170-g277: بدانست is the past single verb 'he/it knew/realized' placed before the subject or epithet (سرخه) — a common classical inversion. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. پایاب اوی is a genitival phrase where پایاب ('paayab', here 'qanat outlet / water outlet') is followed by the archaic possessive اوی = 'his'.
segments.s170-g278: ندارد is the present/past-form auxiliary 'does not have / had not' here meaning 'had no' (third person). غمی گشت uses گشت in the sense 'became' (i.e., 'became sorrow' = 'grew sorrowful'); poetic idiom allows a noun + گشت = 'became X'. و برگاشت روی = 'and he turned/withdrew his face' — برگاشت (took up/removed/turned back) + روی 'face'. The clause sequence describes the emotional reaction.
segments.s170-g279: پس is the connective 'then/thereupon'. اندر before the name (اندر فرامرز) is a classical locative/introducing particle often used to present the subject ('now as for Faramarz' / 'then Faramarz'). با تیغ تیز is the instrumental phrase 'with a sharp sword' (با + instrument, adjective تیز after the noun تیغ).
segments.s170-g280: همی is an archaic/prosodic prefix often marking continuous or general imperfective aspect in poetry (here with a past verb for vividness). تاخت is the past form 'he rushed/charged'. انگیخته is a participle/adjective 'roused/instigated' modifying the following noun رستخیز ('uprising/commotion'), so انگیخته رستخیز = 'having roused an uprising' or 'stirring a great tumult'. The conjunction و simply links the actions.
segments.s170-g321: بسان = a similative preposition 'like/as' introducing a comparison. سیاوش is the name being compared to. سرش را is 'his head' (سر + possessive suffix -ش) with the object marker را after it; ز تن (ز = poetic form of از) means 'from the body'. The whole fragment is the object phrase that the following verb will take: 'his head from the body (they ... )'.
segments.s170-g322: ببرند here is a 3rd-person plural verbal form (poetic/narrative) indicating 'they take/cut off' (used as 'they cut off / take away'); و = 'and'; کرگس is the agent 'vulture'; بپوشد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of پوشیدن used here in the sense 'cover' or 'wrap (over)'; کفن is the thing used, 'shroud'. The second clause completes the action begun in the first: they cut off..., and (then) a vulture covers (it) with a shroud.
segments.s170-g323: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he heard'. طوس سپهبد is an apposition 'Tus, the spahbed (general)'. برفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'he went' (parallel to رفت); it is the main verb of this clause.
segments.s170-g324: به خون ریختن is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to shed/spill blood' or 'for blood-shedding' expressing intent; روی here means 'face' and بنهاد is the past form 'he put/placed' (he assumed), literally 'he put on a face'; تفت is used metaphorically as 'burning/scorching'—so روی بنهاد تفت means 'he put on a burning/angry look'. Grammatically, the phrase describes his manner or purpose when he went (from the previous clause).
segments.s170-g325: بدو is the dative clitic/phrase 'to him' (meaning 'he said to him'); سرخه is here a proper name (Sorkheh) used as the speaker; گفت is the past tense 'said' (3rd person). ای سرافراز شاه is a vocative: ای 'O', سرافراز 'exalted/lofty', شاه 'king'. The whole segment introduces direct speech.
segments.s170-g326: چه ... ریزی همی is an exclamatory interrogative: چه ... ریزی (here ریزی is a verbal form meaning 'you pour/shed' — 2nd-person present) with همی (an archaic/progressive particle) giving continuous or emphatic aspect ('you are/you would be shedding'). خون من = 'my blood' (خون + possessive من); بی‌گناه = 'innocent'. Together: 'Why are you shedding my innocent blood?'.
segments.s170-g327: سیاوش is the personal name (subject). مرا بود is an older phrasing meaning 'to me he was' (مرا is the dative/accusative 'to me' used with بود); بود is the past copula 'was'. هم‌سال 'peer/contemporary' and دوست 'friend' are predicate nouns joined by و. The clause states the relation: 'Siavash was, to me, a contemporary and friend.'
segments.s170-g328: روانم 'my soul/spirit' (روان + possessive -م); پر از = 'full of' (پر 'full', از 'of/from'); درد و اندوه = 'pain and sorrow' (dard = pain/suffering, اندوه = sorrow); اوست is 'it is because of him' / 'it is he'—here the copula ست (poetic variant of است) with the pronoun او indicates the cause/target ('for him' / 'because of him'). The clause describes the speaker's inner state caused by Siavash.
segments.s170-g9: Word order is subject phrase then predicative complement: 'همه شهر توران' (the whole city of Turan) is the topic, followed by the adjective phrase 'پر از داغ و درد' (lit. 'full of grief and pain'). 'پر' is used predicatively with the preposition 'از' to mean 'full of.' This is a simple past/state description of condition (no separate finite verb is needed here).
segments.s170-g10: 'به بیشه درون' is a locative phrase: 'به' introduces the location (here 'into/in the'), 'بیشه' = grove and 'درون' reinforces interiority ('within'). Word order is preposition → place → modifier → noun ('برگ گلنار' = leaves of the pomegranate). Finally 'زرد' is predicate-adjective 'yellow.' The clause describes a local scene that parallels the first clause.
segments.s170-g11: The verb 'گرفتند' (past plural) appears before its object 'شیون' — a common poetic/order-flexible order in classical Persian meaning 'they took up wailing' or 'wailing seized (the place).' 'به هر کوهسار' is a locative phrase ('on/at every mountainside'), with 'هر' modifying 'کوهسار.' The sense is collective: cries spread over the hills.
segments.s170-g12: A negative correlative construction: 'نه ... و نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'فریادرس بود' uses the past of 'بود' to state existence in the past ('there was a rescuer'); the negation reverses it: 'نه فریادرس بود' = 'there was no rescuer', and 'نه خواستار' continues the negation with the second noun ('nor any petitioner').
segments.s170-g13: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal subordinate clause. 'این گفته' = 'this statement/report' is the object of the verb 'بشنید' ('he heard'); note the verb precedes the subject in the line ('بشنید کاووس شاه'), a poetic/older word order where the verb comes before its subject. The subject is 'کاووس شاه' (King Kavus).
segments.s170-g14: 'سر نامدارش نگون شد ز گاه' is a compact predicate: 'سر نامدارش' = 'his famed head' (metaphorically his position/honor), 'نگون شد' is the past passive/inactional result 'became overthrown/ruined' (نگون = downfallen/overthrown), and 'ز گاه' = 'from that time' (ز = from, گاه = time). The phrase states a change of state beginning at that moment.
segments.s170-g15: This is a sequence of parallel actions joined by 'و'. 'بر و جامه بدرید' = 'he tore (his) cloak and garments' — 'بر' here is an archaic/nounal use meaning an outer garment (poetic usage). 'بدرید' is past (they/he tore). 'و رخ را بکند' follows: 'رخ را' (object-marker 'را' marks 'face') and 'بکند' is an older-form past/present of a verb meaning 'he tore/pulled (at)' — the construction shows two physical acts of mourning in succession.
segments.s170-g16: 'به خاک اندر آمد' = 'he came into (the) earth/ground' (a poetic way to say 'came down to the ground'). 'به' introduces the destination, 'خاک' = earth, and 'اندر آمد' is the past tense compound 'entered/came in.' 'ز تخت بلند' ('from the high throne') gives the point of departure with the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' and 'تخت بلند' = 'the high throne.'
segments.s170-g201: بیفگند is an archaic past-tense form (third-person singular) of افکندن, 'he threw/cast.' It takes an omitted direct object here. بر خاک is a prepositional phrase 'onto the ground' (preposition بر + noun خاک). و آمد فرود is a poetic word order: آمد (past 'came') with فرود (nominal/adverbial 'alighting/descent') placed after it; normally one expects فرود آمد, but Ferdowsi inverts for meter — together meaning 'he alit' or 'he came down.'
segments.s170-g202: سیاووش را: را is the Persian definite/direct-object marker marking Siyâvush as the recipient/target. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' چندی modifies درود and here means 'some/several' or 'a few' (contextually 'several' greetings). درود is the thing given — 'greeting(s)/salutation(s)'. The subject is omitted (carried over from prior clause).
segments.s170-g203: سر نامور is a nominative/direct-object phrase 'the famed head' placed before the verb for emphasis. دور کرد is a compound: دور (away/removed) + کرد (past 'did/made') = 'he removed/put away.' از تنش is a prepositional phrase 'from his body' (از + تن + possessive suffix ش). The whole clause means 'he removed the famed head from his body.'
segments.s170-g204: پر از خون is a descriptive phrase 'full of blood' (پر + از + noun). بیالود here is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'stained/bedaubed' (an archaic/poetic form related to آلودن) that modifies پیراهنش 'his shirt' (پیراهن + possessive ش). Word order is adjective/participle before the noun for stylistic effect: 'his shirt, stained full of blood.'
segments.s170-g205: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus; گفت is the past of گفتن 'he said' — here used as the speech introductory verb). کاینت (کاین + ت) is a demonstrative/relative 'that which' or 'this/that' referring to the matter just described; سر کین = 'the head of vengeance' (a genitive phrase); نخست = 'first/at first' — this whole phrase sets up the content of what he said: 'that (head of) vengeance — first ...'.
segments.s170-g206: پراگنده شد is a passive/inchoative construction 'was scattered' or 'became dispersed' (پراگنده = scattered/widespread; شد = became/was). تخم پرخاش is a noun phrase 'the seed of outrage/aggression' (تخم = seed; پرخاش = attack/reproach/aggression) and و رست attaches the second verb: رست = past of رستن 'sprouted/grew.' Together: 'the seed of outrage was scattered and then grew.'
segments.s170-g207: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/the whole.' بوم و بر is a fixed paired phrase meaning 'homeland and land/realm' (بوم = home, بر here used as 'land/region' in the idiom); together they form the object. آتش is 'fire.' اندرفگند is an archaic past plural/third‑person plural form (اندر + افگندن) meaning 'they cast/hurled into' or 'they plunged into.' The implied plural subject ('they') is omitted but understood from the verb.
segments.s170-g208: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'continually/kept' (marks ongoing action). دود 'smoke' is the subject of برشد, an intransitive past form 'rose up' (برشد = it rose/upwent). به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high sky/heaven' (به = to; چرخ = sky/heaven; بلند = high) with the adjective following the noun in typical poetic order.
segments.s170-g233: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. سراسر شد آراسته is a copular sequence: سراسر (’entire/wholly’) + شد (past of شدن, here 'became') + آراسته (adjective 'adorned/arrayed'), i.e. 'became wholly arrayed.' Word order places the adjective phrase after the copula in this poetic register.
segments.s170-g234: بریشان is the contracted prepositional phrase بر + ایشان ('on/against them') used adverbially. پراگنده شد is a passive/inchoative-like construction 'became scattered' (پراگنده = 'scattered', شد = 'became/happened'). خواسته follows as a noun/nominal: 'a wish/desire' (here 'the wish/desire (to do X) arose'). The whole segment reads: 'a wish to scatter upon them became (i.e. arose).'
segments.s170-g235: بزد is simple past 'he struck/beat.' کوس is the object 'war-drum.' رویین and هندی are coordinated modifiers of کوس (two kinds/styles of drum: 'brazen/iron' and 'Indian'); و is the coordinator 'and.' درای is an adjectival/attributive form meaning 'possessing/held' (here functioning as an epithet attached to the preceding noun phrase in poetic compression). Word order places modifiers after the noun in this line for metrical effect.
segments.s170-g236: سواران is the (now) subject 'the horsemen.' سوی + رزم is a prepositional phrase 'toward battle' (سوی = 'toward(s)', رزم = 'battle/fight'). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' and here combines with رای (an object) to form the idiom رای کردند 'they took counsel / made a plan.' The verb-final position follows Persian SOV tendencies, but رای precedes کرد(ند) as a typical object of 'کردن.'
segments.s170-g237: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander.' از introduces a source phrase 'from.' گنگ here is used adjectivally 'muffled/silent' (poetically suggesting a hushed place or the fold of ranks). بیرون 'out' complements گنگ to give 'out of the muffled/silent place.' کشید is the simple past 'drew/pulled' with سپهدار as agent; the object of کشید is implied or continues in the next segment (the movement of the host).
segments.s170-g238: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by the object marker را. ز تنگی به هامون is a source–goal phrase: ز تنگی 'from the narrowness / cramped place' and به هامون 'to the plain' (هامون = 'plain'). کشید repeats the past 'drew/pulled' and takes سپه را as its object: 'he drew the army from the narrow place to the plain.'
segments.s170-g239: فرستاد is past 'he sent.' و is the simple coordinator 'and.' مر سرخه here functions as a proper name (Mar‑Sorkheh) and is followed by را, the object marker (را marks the person summoned). پیش خواند is a compound: پیش 'before/in the presence of' + خواند 'he called/summoned' (خواند here = 'to call/summon' rather than 'to read'). The sequence thus means 'he sent and summoned Mar‑Sorkheh before him.'
segments.s170-g240: ز Rِستم introduces source/topic 'about Rostam' (ز = 'concerning/from'). بسی is an intensive/quantifying word 'many/much' in this context (poetic usage of بسی = 'a great many'). داستانها is the plural 'tales/stories.' براند here is a verb meaning 'he produced/related' (in classical usage it conveys 'brought forth' or 'told'); syntactically it closes the clause with the verb following its objects, a common poetic order.
segments.s170-g177: 'رده' (ranks/rows) is the noun head of the clause; 'بر کشید' is a past action meaning 'drew up' or 'pulled up' (the preverb بر here combines with کشید to indicate upward/into-order action). 'از دو رویه سپاه' is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from the two sides/flanks of the host' that specifies where the ranks were formed; word order is typical poetic Persian with the locating phrase following the verb.
segments.s170-g178: 'به سر' ('onto the head') is a prepositional phrase marking the target of the placing action; 'بر نهادند' is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن, 'they placed/put (upon)', with the particle بر reinforcing the 'upon' sense. 'ز آهن کلاه' means 'a helmet of iron' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic register, followed by material then the noun). The verb is transitive and the noun phrase follows the prepositional target in typical order.
segments.s170-g179: 'ز هر سو' = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = each side); 'برآمد' is the past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'; 'ز گردان خروش' is a causal/source phrase 'a cry/roar from the troop(s)' — note the repeated ز marking source and the poetic placement of modifiers after the verb.
segments.s170-g180: 'همی' is an emphatic/progressive particle (here giving immediacy/continuity); 'کر شد' is 'became deaf' (کر = deaf, شد = became); the phrase 'از ناله کوس گوش' gives the cause/agent ('the ear was made deaf from the drum's lament'), with 'از' marking the cause and 'گوش' the affected organ—word order places the cause before the final noun for poetic effect.
segments.s170-g181: 'چو' = 'when'; 'آواز کوس آمد' = 'the sound/voice of the drum came' (آواز + instrument noun + آمد = the instrument sounded); 'و کرنای' connects 'and the trumpet' (کرنای is the horn/trumpet, here left to link into the next clause). The clause uses concise, poetic coordination rather than a full clause for the trumpet.
segments.s170-g182: 'فرامرز را' places Faramarz as the topical/experiencer marked by را; 'دل برآمد' ('the heart rose') is an idiom meaning 'his heart leapt' or 'he was stirred/overcome'; 'ز جای' ('from its place') completes the idiom—برآمد is past tense and دل is the grammatical subject (the heart as the thing that 'rose').
segments.s170-g183: به یک حمله is an adverbial phrase of manner: به marks 'by/with' and یک حمله ('one attack') is indefinite; together 'with a single attack'. اندر ز is an archaic prepositional cluster meaning roughly 'out of/from among' (اندر = in/among; ز = from). گردان هزار = 'the troop/column of a thousand' — note the classical word order where the noun گردان precedes the numeral هزار (numeral postmodifies the noun).
segments.s170-g184: بیفگند is an archaic epic past (3rd person) of افکندن with the verbal prefix ب-, here best rendered 'scattered' or 'threw into disorder'; the direct object is omitted but understood from context. و is the coordinating 'and' linking two past verbs. برگشت is a past (preterite) verb 'he returned/withdrew'. از کارزار is a source phrase with از 'from' + کارزار 'battlefield/battle', giving 'withdrew from the battlefield.'
segments.s170-g249: This is an imperative sequence. کنون 'now' sets the time; پیش رو باش is literally 'be front-facing/at the front' (پیش + رو) — a poetic way to tell someone to take the lead. و links the commands, and بیدار باش is the repeated imperative 'be alert/awake.' The verb باش is the ordinary imperative 'be.'
segments.s170-g250: سپه را uses را as the definite object marker ('the army'); ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/against.' نگهدار باش is an imperative compound: نگهدار functions as 'keep/guard' and is strengthened by باش 'be' (together 'be a keeper/guard'); the clause means 'guard the army from the enemy.'
segments.s170-g251: ز پیش پدر = 'in front of / before his father' (poetic placement). سرخه is the adjective 'crimson' placed before the verb for poetic effect and effectively modifies the standard mentioned next. بیرون کشید is the past tense 'pulled/drew out' (poetic word order separates adjective and its noun).
segments.s170-g252: درفش و سپه را = 'the standard and the army' with را marking the definite object. به هامون کشید is 'drew/led (them) to the plain' — به هامون 'into/toward the plain.' کشید is past 'drew/led' (here used transitively).
segments.s170-g253: چو here = 'when.' طلایه = 'the vanguard.' گرد سپه is used as 'the dust/dust-cloud of the army' (poetic compound), and دید is the simple past 'saw.' تفت is a proper name in this context (hence not a regular verbal sense).
segments.s170-g254: بپیچید is past 'turned' (the troop or person turned). و links the actions; سوی = 'toward,' and رفت is the simple past 'went.' The phrase thus describes a turn and movement toward the named person Faramarz.
segments.s170-g255: از ایران سپه برشد is a compact, poetic past clause: از ایران 'from Iran,' سپه 'the army,' برشد 'rose up' (a poetic past form meaning 'arose' or 'advanced'). آوای کوس = 'the sound/cry of the drum' (juxtaposed as a second short image).
segments.s170-g256: ز گرد سپه = 'from the army's dust' (ز = 'from/of,' گرد = 'dust/cloud' here). شد + predicate (هوا آبنوس) is 'the air became ebony/black' — شد is the copular/causative 'became' and آبنوس describes the resulting dark color of the air.
segments.s170-g265: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. The noun phrase سرخه بران گونه پیگار is the object of دید; سرخه ('crimson') modifies گونه ('cheek'), and بران is a poetic/linking particle placing 'crimson' over 'cheek' (literally 'crimson-on-cheek'), so the phrase personifies the battle as 'the battle whose cheeks were crimson'. دید is the simple past 'saw'.
segments.s170-g266: درفش فرامرز سالار is a compact noun phrase: درفش 'standard/banner' with the name فرامرز followed by the title سالار 'commander' in apposition. دید again is the past tense 'he saw' repeating the action for emphasis (a common poetic parallelism).
segments.s170-g267: عنان را is the direct object ('the reins') marked by را. به + بور سرافراز is the indirect/recipient phrase 'to the fair (بور), lofty / triumphant (سرافراز)' — the two adjectives form a compound epithet (likely of a steed). داد is the past 'gave' (here 'handed over').
segments.s170-g268: به نیزه درآمد is a verbal phrase where درآمد (literary past) means 'turned to / took up' and به نیزه specifies the instrument/role 'to the spear' (i.e. he took up the spear). کمان باز داد follows in parallel and means 'he made the bow ready / drew the bow' — باز here has the sense 'open/ready' and داد is again the past 'gave' used idiomatically ('set the bow ready' or 'drew his bow').
segments.s170-g269: فرامرز is the subject (proper name). بگذاشت is the past tense 'placed/put' (the b- prefix is a literary/formal past). قلب سپاه is the object 'the heart/core of the army' (noun + noun acting as genitive), so the clause reads 'Faramarz placed the army's core...'.
segments.s170-g270: بر سرخه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against the (one) called Serkheh' (بر = 'on/against'); in context سرخه functions as a descriptor/name. با نیزه is the instrumental 'with a spear'. شد is the inchoative past 'became' and کینه‌خواه is the predicate noun 'avenger' (so 'with a spear he became an avenger').
segments.s170-g271: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / a certain man'. نیزه زد is the verb phrase 'struck with a spear' (نیزه object/instrument, زد past 'struck'). همچو آذرگشسپ is a simile placed after the verb: 'like Azar‑Gashasp' (a comparison to that heroic figure).
segments.s170-g272: ز کوهه is the prepositional phrase 'from the mountain' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). ببردش is a transitive verbal form meaning 'he carried/took him away' with the enclitic -ش = 'him'. سوی یال اسپ gives the direction 'toward the mane of the horse' (سوی = 'toward', یال 'mane' governed by اسپ 'horse' in a noun–noun possessive).
segments.s170-g65: This is an exclamatory nominal phrase: دریغ (an interjection 'pity/alas') heads the line and is followed by the demonstrative + epithet آن گو نامبرده سوار = 'that so‑called/named rider'. گو here functions like a 'called/so‑said' marker attached to نامبرده; word order places the demonstrative and modifiers before the head noun.
segments.s170-g66: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the lament ('that ...'). چون او = 'like him' (چون as comparative), نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive/optative of دیدن used here to mean 'does not/will not see' (i.e. 'there will be none who see/meet one like him'), and دگر روزگار = 'another of the age/times' (object of نبیند). Word order is typical Persian: subordinate particle + comparative phrase + verb + object.
segments.s170-g67: چو = temporal 'when'; در بزم بودی = 'you were in the assembly/banquet' (بودى = second‑person past). بهاران here is the thing 'given' or the metaphorical result ('spring' = brightness, festivity) and بدی functions as an archaic/poetic past 'you gave' (this verbal sense is not among the provided senses for بدی, so it is marked below as -1). The clause is a simple temporal clause describing the hero's behavior in the feast.
segments.s170-g68: به رزم = 'in/at the battle' (به as locative/temporal), افسر نامداران is the direct object 'helmets (or crests) of/the renowned ones' and the final بدی is the same archaic/past 'you gave/bestowed' as above (again not matched in the provided senses). The line parallels the previous clause: generosity or furnishing in feast and in war.
segments.s170-g69: همی + verb gives continuous or emphatic aspect ('I shall/ever do'); جنگ ... کنم = 'I will wage war' with کنم as the 1st‑person present/subjunctive used for future/intent. با چشم گریان is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with tearful eyes' (گریان is a present participle/adjectival 'weeping').
segments.s170-g70: جهان at the front is the object 'the world'; چون = 'as/like' introduces the simile 'as (I have) my own heart ...'; دل خویش is 'his/her/one's own heart' with خوی + ش forming the possessive notion (here خوی carries the notion of kin/own and ش = 'his/her'), بریان = 'burnt/scorched' and کنم repeats the volitional 'I will make/ I will do'. The clause means 'I will scorch/lay waste the world as I have scorched my own heart.'
