segments.s170-g71: نگه کرد is a compound verb 'to cast a look/gaze' with نگه (gaze) + کرد (did). کاووس is the agent; بر چهر او = 'upon his face' (بر + object). Word order places the light noun نگه before the verb, a common classical pattern.
segments.s170-g72: بدید = 'he saw' (perfect/past). اشک خونین = 'a blood‑red tear' (خونین as adjectival 'blood‑stained/blood‑red'); و آن مهر او = 'and that love/affection of his' (مهر here = 'love/affection'). The verb governs the two coordinated objects; possessive او follows the noun as is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s170-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a light, literal sequence 'information/news came' meaning 'news arrived'. به + NP (به کاووس شاه) marks the indirect object/recipient 'to King Kavus'; note the title شاه follows the name as an appositive/title.
segments.s170-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. شد is the past of شدن 'became/happened' used as a copular/resultive verb. روزگار سیاوش is a genitive construction 'Siyâvash's time/fate' serving as the grammatical subject, and تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined'.
segments.s170-g3: به کردار مرغان is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/way of the birds' (کردار here = 'manner/behavior'). سرش را is the definite direct object 'his head' (سر + pronominal suffix ش, with object marker را). ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' (ز = 'from', poetic form).
segments.s170-g4: جدا کرد is the verb phrase 'separated / cut off' with جدا ('apart') preceding the verb for emphasis. سالار آن انجمن is the subject 'the chief/leader of that assembly' and appears after the verb — a poetic inversion where the verb comes before the agent.
segments.s170-g5: ابر here is literal 'cloud' used poetically (in context it functions as a collective/poetic image referring to people/agents). بی‌گناهش is 'his innocent one' or 'the innocent (person) of his' (بی‌گناه + pronominal suffix ش). به خنجر uses به as instrumental 'with/by a dagger' and the second به زار forms an adverbial phrase 'in a wretched/pitiable manner' (زار = 'wretched/lamentable'), so the whole cluster gives instrument + manner.
segments.s170-g6: بریدند is the past plural 'they cut/they severed' (agent plural implied). سر زان تن شاهوار is the object phrase 'the head from that/this lofty (شاهوار) body' where زان = 'from that' links head and body, and شاهوار is an epithet 'regal/lofty' modifying تن.
segments.s170-g7: بنالد (from نالیدن) is a poetic form meaning 'wailed/lamely lamented'; combined with همی it indicates continuous or habitual action 'kept lamenting'. بلبل is the subject 'the nightingale'. از شاخ سرو is a locative/origin phrase 'from the bough of the cypress' (شاخ = 'bough/branch', سرو = 'cypress').
segments.s170-g8: چو is a comparative/temporal 'as/when'. دراج in this poetic context refers to a small bird (swift/dove-like); the lexical senses listed are non-bird meanings, so here it is used metaphorically/poetically as a bird. زیر گلان is 'under the blossoms' (زیر = under, گلان = flowers/blossoms). با تذرو is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with tazru' (تذرو = to scatter/beat about, here 'scattering/fluttering'), giving the manner of the dove's movement.
segments.s170-g281: سواران ایران is a genitive noun phrase: سواران (horsemen/riders) modified by ایران (of Iran) = 'the horsemen of Iran'. به کردار is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/guise of' and takes the following noun دیو as the complement: 'in the form/appearance of a demon.' Word order is noun + modifier then the simile introduced by به کردار.
segments.s170-g282: This line continues the description: دمان (lit. 'hem/skirt', here forming part of the compound with the previous دیو to mean 'demon-clad') follows the demon-epithet. از پسش is a prepositional phrase 'from/behind him' (پسش = پشتِ او). برکشیده is a past participle/verb 'raised' and governs غریو (the shout/clamor) as what was raised; poetic order places the instrument/adjunct before the verb.
segments.s170-g283: چون here functions as a temporal/concessive conjunction 'when/as'. سرخه را uses the object marker را, so 'Sorkheh' is the direct object of یافت (یافتن). یافت is past tense 'found/seized' and takes چنگ ('claw, talon') as the instrument/indication of the attack: 'he caught/struck with his claw.'
segments.s170-g284: بیازید is a past-form of یازیدن/یاختن meaning 'broke forth/sprung' and introduces the action; زان سان که is a comparative conjunction 'in such a manner that' (زان = 'from that/therefore', سان = 'manner/so'), and the clause یازد پلنگ gives the simile 'as the panther leapt.' The whole phrase sets up a simile comparing Farāmarz's sudden spring to a panther's leap.
segments.s170-g285: گرفتش is the past tense verb گرفت with the 3rd-person object clitic -ش ('he seized him/it'); here the following noun کمربند ('belt') specifies how he gripped him — 'seized him by the belt.' و links the next phrase; از پشت زین is a locative phrase 'from behind the saddle' indicating the place/direction of the action.
segments.s170-g286: برآورد is a verbal form (بر + آوردن) here meaning 'he lifted/raised/pulled up' (poetic use); و زد is 'and he struck' (زد past of زدن). ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly', and بر زمین is the result/directional phrase 'onto the ground' showing where the struck person fell.
segments.s170-g287: پیاده here refers to the foot-soldier (the man on foot) who is the one cast down; به پیش 'before/in front (of)' indicates direction/position relative to others. اندر افگند is the past verb 'cast/threw in' (poetic word order), and خوار is an adjective 'humiliated/despised' describing the state in which he was thrown down.
segments.s170-g288: به لشکرگه is a prepositional phrase 'to the army-camp' (destination). آوردش is آورد + object clitic -ش 'he brought him', with -ش referring back to the cast-down man; از کارزار 'from the battlefield' gives the origin — he was brought from the fight into the camp.
segments.s170-g225: نماند is a literary past-form meaning "was left / remained (not)" and here with the following ایچ (an emphatic "nothing") gives a negative existential: "nothing remained." بر دشت is a locative phrase "on the plain." ز + noun (ز اسپان) functions poetically to mark source/agent "from/of the horses," and یله (postpositive adjective) modifies اسپان as "loose/unrestrained horses." The word order (verb + negative pronoun + locative + from-phrase + noun + adjective) is typical of classical Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s170-g226: بیاورد is the literary/past third-person singular of آوردن ("brought/drove"), here introducing what the shepherd did. چوپان is the explicit subject that follows the verb (common in poetry). به میدان is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning "into/to the field/arena," and گله is the object "flock/herd" (placed at the end). The clause thus reads literally "(he) brought the flock into the field" with verb-subject-object order shaped by poetic diction.
segments.s170-g227: در گنج is a simple locative "in the treasury/vault." The following coordinated nouns (گوپال و برگستوان) are items or names listed as contents; in this line گوپال is best read as a proper name (Gopala) while برگستوان appears as a lexical item used as a name here (the provided lemma senses do not include a proper-name reading, so it is treated as a nominal item in context). The sequence is a short noun phrase listing contents of the treasury.
segments.s170-g228: همان is a resumptive demonstrative meaning "the same / likewise," linking this list to the previous items. The phrase نیزه و خنجر هندوان is a coordinated noun phrase "the spear and the dagger of the Indians," with هندوان ("Indians") marking possession/association; word order is noun1 + و + noun2 + possessor (typical of Persian noun groups in poetry).
segments.s170-g229: همان again resumes/list-continues: "the same" or "likewise." گنج governs the list of treasure items that follow. دینار is "dinars (coins)," و coordinates items, and گهر is "gems/jewels." The item در in this position appears as an item name (likely a kind of jewel/pearl in the poetic catalogue) rather than the preposition "in" — the given lemma senses don't include that specific lexical sense, so it is left uncertain for the dictionary senses. The whole segment is an enumerative noun phrase.
segments.s170-g230: همان again resumes the catalogue. افسر here is the classical word for a helmet (not modern "officer"). طوق is a neck-ring/torc or collar; زرین is the adjective "golden," placed before کمر which here means the "girdle/belt." Adjective-noun order (زرین کمر) is normal; the coordinated sequence lists armature and ornaments kept with the treasure.
segments.s170-g231: ز دستور is a prepositional phrase "by/on (the) order (of) ..."; گنجور is the agent/possessive noun meaning "treasury/treasurer" (here 'the treasury' or its keeper). بستد is the past third-person singular of بستن "to close/lock," and کلید is the direct object "the key." The clause therefore reads literally "by the treasury's order he locked the key," with the agent phrase fronted (typical poetical word order).
segments.s170-g232: همه quantifies the coordinated nouns کاخ و میدان as "all the palaces and fields." درم is the object "dirhams," and گسترید is the past third-person singular of گستراندن (here passive/active sense "scattered/strewn/spread" in classical narrative). The order places the quantified locations first and then the object+verb (درم گسترید) — a poetic ordering meaning "dirhams were scattered over all palaces and fields."
segments.s170-g153: سزد‌گر is a conditional/optative particle here meaning “if it be fitting.” بگویی is a subjunctive/optative form ‘that you say’ (content clause introduced by گر implicit in سزدگر). مرا + را is the object construction ‘to me’ (مرا = me with را marking it definite/object). نام خویش is a noun + possessive cluster: نام ‘name’ followed by خوی + ش serving to indicate ‘your/one’s own name’. Word order reflects classical Persian: verb (بگویی) before its object phrase.
segments.s170-g154: بجویی is a second‑person subjunctive/optative meaning ‘you should seek’ or ‘seek (for yourself)’; it expresses the action the speaker advises. ازین = از + این (‘from/ of this’), where ین is the orthographic variant of این. کار فرجام خویش is a genitival sequence ‘the outcome/end of this affair for yourself’ (فرجام = end/outcome; خویش + ش again marks the reflexive/possessive).
segments.s170-g155: همانا is an emphatic adverb ‘truly/verily’ placed at the front. به فرمان شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘by the king’s command’ (به + noun + possessor). آمدی is past 2nd person singular ‘you came’ and completes the clause; the clause states a fact about the addressee’s arrival.
segments.s170-g156: گر is the conditional particle ‘if’. از پهلوان سپاه is a genitival phrase where از ‘from/out of’ combines with پهلوان سپاه ‘a champion of the host/army’ (پهلوان modified by سپاه). آمدی repeats the 2nd‑person past verb ‘you came’, presenting an alternative or hypothetical provenance of the visitor.
segments.s170-g157: چه داری is the interrogative ‘what do you have/what news have you’ (چه = what; داری = present/possessive ‘you have’). ز افراسیاب آگهی is a prepositional phrase ‘information about Afrasiab’ (ز = of/about; آگهی = news/information) and functions as the complement to داری.
segments.s170-g158: This is a coordinated list introduced by repeated ز ‘of/about’ for poetic emphasis: ز اورنگ و ز تاج و تخت مهی = ‘of the throne, and of the crown, and of the moon‑like seat’. مهی is an adjective modifying تخت (‘moon‑like/beautiful throne’). The repeated ز marks each item as object of the preceding question.
segments.s170-g159: نباید is an impersonal modal ‘it must not / one should not’. که introduces the complement clause. بی‌نام is a predicative/epithet ‘nameless’ describing the (understood) addressee; بر دست من is a locative/goal phrase ‘into my hand’ (بر + دست with من as possessor), literally ‘upon my hand’ meaning ‘into my power’. The clause expresses prohibition/undesirability of that outcome.
segments.s170-g160: روانت is روان + second‑person suffix -ت ‘your soul/spirit’. برآید is here a potential/optative sense ‘would rise/depart’ (literally ‘would go up’). ز تاریک تن is a prepositional phrase ‘from the dark body’ (ز = from; تاریک تن = dark body), giving the consequence: your soul would leave your dark body.
segments.s170-g145: ورازاد is used as a proper name (appearing first). بد نام is a postpositive adjective (bad‑named / ill‑famed) modifying the name; in epic Persian adjectival phrases often follow the noun. آن پهلوان is a demonstrative + noun (‘that champion’), with آن functioning like the definite/article/demonstrative that picks out the hero.
segments.s170-g146: A string of epithets joined by و. دلیر is a straightforward adjective ‘brave’. سپه تاز is a compound built from سپه (army) + تاز (fierce/charger) meaning ‘one who storms/charges the host’. روشن‌روان is a compound adjective ‘bright‑souled / fair‑souled’; the sequence lists qualities of the same hero.
segments.s170-g147: سپه بود is a simple past copular construction ‘there was an army’. شمشیرزن سی هزار places the noun before the numeral for poetic effect: ‘thirty thousand swordsmen’ (predicate of the copula). بود is the past tense of بودن.
segments.s170-g148: همه quantifies the following description. رزم‌جوی is a compound meaning ‘battle‑seeking / eager for combat’ (رزم = battle, جوی = seeking). از در کارزار is a prepositional phrase giving the place/context (‘on/in the field of battle’), where از takes a poetic spatial sense and در means ‘in/within’ the کارزار (battlefield).
segments.s170-g149: ورازاد is the subject (proper name). از قلب لشکر is a prepositional phrase ‘from the heart (center) of the army’ (قلب لشکر = centre of the host). برفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning ‘he went/left’ (classical past of رفتن; here used in narrative past).
segments.s170-g150: بیامد is a past tense verb ‘he came’. به نزد is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to the presence of / to’ (نزد here = in the presence of). فرامرز تفت appears as a personal name plus epithet; تفت functions as part of the name/epithet rather than its literal verbal meaning.
segments.s170-g151: بپرسید is a past narrative form ‘he asked’. و links clauses. گفتش is ‘he said to him’ (گفت + enclitic ش = to him). چه مردی بگوی is an imperative/request-like formula: چه مردی ‘what kind of man (are you)’ and بگوی is an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘say / tell’ (here introducing the direct question).
segments.s170-g152: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’. کرده‌ای is the present perfect/past-present form ‘you have done / you have made’ used here with روی to mean ‘you have turned/come’. سوی is a preposition ‘toward’. این مرز is ‘this border/frontier’. روی here (with کردن/کرده‌ای) means ‘to turn one’s face toward’ — idiomatically ‘to come/appear at’ the border.
segments.s170-g241: بدو (bedo) is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb (archaic/poetic order). گفت is the past form 'said' (the senses list given doesn't include this exact meaning, so treat it as the reporting verb). شمشیرزن is the agent 'the swordsman' and here follows the verb phrase (inverted word order typical of older Persian: 'to him said the swordsman').
segments.s170-g242: This is the reported command: ببر is the imperative 'take!' (addressing someone to take/toward action). سی هزار modifies what is to be taken, and نامدار qualifies them as 'renowned'. از در کارزار is a classical locution: از + در here marks movement into/onto the arena of fighting (literary phrasing for 'into the field of battle').
segments.s170-g243: نگه‌دار here functions as an exhortation 'guard/keep' (imperative sense), followed by جان 'life/soul' as its object. از introduces the threat 'from/against', and بد پور زال is a genitive phrase 'the bad/ill son of Zal' (پور = son/descendant; زال is the name).
segments.s170-g244: به رزمت is 'in/for your battle' (به + رزمت = within your fighting/host). نباشد is a jussive/optative negative 'let there not be' or 'there should be no...', followed by جزو کس همال 'a member/person equal (to him)': جزو 'member/part', کس 'anyone/person', همال here 'peer/equal' (figurative). The clause says: 'let there be no one in your ranks who is his equal.'
segments.s170-g245: تو is the subject 'you'. فرزندی is a predicative noun 'a child/offspring' (you are my child). و links the second predicate; نیکخواه 'well-wisher' is another predicative noun. من + ی (the copular suffix) yields 'of mine / you are (to) me' — in classical Persian the copula often appears as this enclitic.
segments.s170-g246: ستون سپاهی is a metaphorical epithet: ستون 'pillar/support' + سپاهی 'of the army' (here adjectival), meaning 'pillar/support of the army'. و links the next epithet ماه منی 'you are my moon' (ماه used metaphorically for beauty/glory). The final ی is the same copular suffix marking predication.
segments.s170-g247: چو is the conditional/temporal 'when/if'; بیدار دل 'alert heart' is an adjectival phrase (your heart being awake). باشی is the present/subjunctive form 'you are' (here used in the protasis 'if/when you are...'). راه‌جوی is a compound noun 'road-seeker' or 'traveler/pursuer of the path', completing the condition.
segments.s170-g248: که introduces the apodosis/relative sense 'so that/then' or 'that' linking the following instruction. یارد 'companion/helper' is the object to be placed (نهادن 'to place/put'). بروی is the second-person singular present/subjunctive 'you go' (command-like or consequential). تو is the explicit pronoun for emphasis. روی here is a noun 'face/front' used adverbially ('forward/in front') in this poetic line—so the sequence says roughly 'put a companion in place and you go forward.'
segments.s170-g305: چو is the conjunction 'when'; این گوهران is the direct object 'these gems' referring back to the previously mentioned jewels; را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to the NP. بجا آورد is a light verbal phrase: بجا (fit/right place) + آورد (bring/put) — together 'to put in the proper place' or 'to set'. Word order is conjunction → object → object marker → verb phrase.
segments.s170-g306: دلاور شود is 'becomes (a) dīlavar' — شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن 'to become' used here as a simple resultive 'becomes'. پر و پا آورد is an idiomatic, pictorial phrase: پر (feather) و پا (foot) + آورد (bring/produce) — literally 'brings feathers and feet', i.e. 'sprouts wings and feet' or 'becomes vigorous/formidable'. The conjunction و simply links the two nouns inside the idiom.
segments.s170-g307: از آتش 'from/of fire' sets the domain; نبینی is the negative present/optative form of دیدن 'to see' (here general/present sense 'you see/one sees not'); جز introduces the sole exception; افروختن is the infinitive 'kindling/ignition' — together: 'from fire one sees nothing except ignition.'
segments.s170-g308: جهانی is 'a world' / 'the world' as the thing that approaches; چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; پیش آیدش is a verbal cluster: پیش 'before/in front of' + آیدش 'comes to/comes upon it' (آید + -ش 'to it'); سوختن is the infinitive 'to burn' used here as a verblike predicate meaning 'it burns' — 'when a world comes before it, (it) burns.'
segments.s170-g309: فرامرز is the proper name (subject/topic). نشگفت is from شگفت/شگفتن ('to astonish') with a negative sense here ('was not astonished' / 'would not be amazed'); اگر introduces the conditional 'if'; سرکش است is the predicate 'is headstrong/insolent'. The whole clause means 'Farāmarz would not be surprised if he is headstrong.'
segments.s170-g310: که here functions as a causal 'for/because'. پولاد is a personal name (Polād). را after the name has a topical/emphatic/possessive flavour marking 'Polād' as the relevant person; دل 'heart' and پر از آتش 'full of fire' is the predicate with است 'is'. So: 'for Polād's heart is full of fire.'
segments.s170-g311: چو 'when'; آورد here 'brings/strikes' (literal 'brings' used in the sense 'applies/strikes'); با سنگ خارا 'with a flint (stone)' — خارا is a rock/flint noun; کند is the verb 'does/makes' (here the main verb of the clause). The sequence describes the action performed with flint (striking the steel).
segments.s170-g312: ز (poetic form of از) 'from'; دل راز خویش = 'the secret of his own heart' (خویش formed by خوی + ش, with ش = his); آشکارا 'openly/manifestly'; کند 'makes/causes' (here 'reveals'). Together: 'he thereby makes his heart's secret manifest.'
segments.s170-g73: نداد is the past negative of دادن ('did not give'); ایچ is an emphatic negative ('at all' / 'nothing') modifying پاسخ ('answer'). Classical/poetic word order places the object phrase before or adjacent to the verb. مر here is an archaic enclitic dative (used with the full pronoun او را) specifying the indirect object, and ز شرم is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'out of shame' giving the reason.
segments.s170-g74: فرو ریخت is a compound (= فرو + ریخت) meaning 'poured down' / 'fell' (past). از دیدگان is a poetic genitive/ablative 'from the eyes', and آب گرم ('warm water') is a kenning for hot tears; the whole phrase describes tears pouring out of the eyes.
segments.s170-g75: تهمتن is an epithet/name (the mighty one) used as the subject. برفت is an older/poetic past-form meaning 'went up/rose' (here treated as the verb 'rose'); از بر تخت اوی literally 'from upon his throne' uses از + بر + noun to mark movement off of a surface, and اوی is the possessive 'his/her' attached to تخت.
segments.s170-g76: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'; خان here is an honorific/title attached to the name سودابه (Lady Sudabeh). بنهاد is a past verb meaning 'placed/turned' (here governing روی 'face'): the sequence means 'he turned/laid his face toward Khan Sudabeh' (i.e., he faced her).
segments.s170-g77: ز پرده 'from (behind) the curtain' marks origin. به گیسوش ('to/for her hair') uses به as a directional/goal-like preposition + گیسوش ('her hair'). بیرون کشید is the verb 'drew/pulled out' (بیرون 'out' + کشید 'drew'), describing the motion of pulling hair out from the curtain.
segments.s170-g78: ز تخت بزرگیش 'from her great/lofty throne' gives a source/setting (ز + noun + possessive -یش). در خون کشید is a compressed poetic clause: در here is the in/into preposition and خون ('blood'); کشید is the verb 'drew'—together implying that in/beside her throne blood was drawn or hair stained with blood (poetic/compact expression).
segments.s170-g79: به خنجر 'with/by a dagger' uses به in the instrumental sense ('by/with'); به دو نیم کردش is an idiomatic phrasing 'made (it) into two halves' (نیم 'half', کردش 'he made it' where -ش is the object clitic). The final به راه here is idiomatic 'on the spot / at once' or 'toward the way' depending on context—overall the clause says 'he split it into two with a dagger'.
segments.s170-g80: نجنبید is the past negative 'did not move/stir'. بر جای 'from his place' uses بر as 'on/upon' + جای 'place'; کاووس شاه names the person (Kavus the king) who remained motionless. The order places the negated verb before the locative and the name for emphasis, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s170-g97: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('for/that'); اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' (اندر = poetic 'in'). چون سیاوش modifies سوار: چون + name = 'like Siavash', so چُن followed by سیاوش qualifies سوار = 'a rider like Siavash'. Word order is flexible and poetic: modifier (چون سیاوش) appears next to the noun it likens (سوار).
segments.s170-g98: نبندد is the classical-present/optative/3rd-person form with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'does not / would not bind/gird' used here generically. کمر is the body-part/girdle 'waist' (object of بندن/بستن). نیز 'also/likewise' and the phrase یک نامدار ('a single/one renowned') emphasize that not even one famed man does this; in poetry یک can mean 'any/one'. The verb precedes its object for emphasis in this line.
segments.s170-g99: چنین کار = 'such an affair/matter' (چنین here = 'such'). یکسر مدارید is a hortative/imperative-style expression: یکسر 'entirely/wholly' + مدارید (be lenient/treat gently) — the speaker is commanding or advising (addressed to خرد). خرد at the end is a vocative 'wise one' (literally 'wisdom' used to address the wise).
segments.s170-g100: چنین کینه را = 'such a hatred/grudge' with را marking the definite object. خرد is the agent/topic 'wisdom' (what might measure or settle it). نتوان (negated توان, 'cannot') + شمرد (infinitive/3rd-person root 'to count/ reckon') = 'cannot be counted/ reckoned.' Together: 'such a grudge cannot be reckoned by wisdom.'
segments.s170-g101: ز دلها = 'from the hearts' (ز = poetic 'from'); همه ترس = 'all fear' (همه modifies ترس). بیرون کنید is a plural/honorific imperative 'drive out / remove' (بیرون = 'out/away', کنید = 'do/make' used here as the imperative form). The verb follows the object phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s170-g102: زمین را is topical/definite object-marked 'the earth' (را). ز خون = 'with/from blood' (instrumental/material). رود جیحون is the river-name phrase 'the Jihun river' (رود = 'river'); here it functions as the target/extent in the image. کنید (imperative) links back and commands to 'make/let' — poetically: 'make the earth run with blood to the Jihun.'
segments.s170-g103: به یزدان is an oath 'by God' (به used adverbially as 'by'). که introduces the content of the oath. تا در جهان زنده‌ام = 'as long as I am alive in the world': تا = 'as long as/until', در جهان = locative 'in the world', زنده‌ام = present 'I am alive' (زنده + enclitic ام = 'I am').
segments.s170-g104: به کین سیاوش = 'with/for the vengeance of Siavash' (به here = 'for/with' introducing the object of feeling). دل آگنده‌ام = 'my heart is filled' (آگنده‌ام = 'I am filled/stained with' with دل as the affected noun); word order places the object-of-feeling phrase before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s170-g313: به سرخه نگه کرد is the verb phrase ‘to look/turn one’s gaze to Serkhe’: به + place, نگه (gaze) + کردن (کرد, past) = 'looked toward'. پس = 'then' introduces what follows; پیلتن is a name/place placed after پس (poetic order). The past auxiliary کرد is a simple past (3rd singular) used with the light noun نگه to form 'looked'.
segments.s170-g314: This is a nominal-existential clause with an archaic past copula: یکی = 'one/a certain'; سرو آزاده = 'a cypress (sīnak) described as noble/proud'; بد here is an archaic form of the copula بود (i.e. بُد = 'was'), so the clause reads 'there was one noble cypress on the meadow'. بر چمن is the locative 'on the lawn'.
segments.s170-g315: برش is a contracted locative/profile word (بر + -ش) meaning 'on him' or 'his bearing/profile'; چون ... چون marks two similes: 'چون بر شیر' = 'as (a) lion' and 'رخ چون بهار' = 'his face like spring'. There is no explicit copula here (classical Persian often omits بود), so these are predicate similes.
segments.s170-g316: ز + instrument (مشک) introduces the means: 'with/from musk'. سیه کرده is a compound: سیه (black/dark) + کرده (past participle of کردن) = 'having made dark' or 'darkened'. بر گل نگار is the target 'upon the rose-like cheek' (گل نگار = rosy/beautiful cheek). Together: 'having darkened the rosy cheek with black musk.'
segments.s170-g317: بفرمود is an honorific/simple past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. پس 'then' links temporally. تا introduces the subordinate/jussive clause (classical use = 'that' / 'so that') and برندش is a 3rd-person-plural jussive/subjunctive 'let them carry/take him' (برند + -ش object suffix). به دشت = 'to the plain'.
segments.s170-g318: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic form of با ('with') introducing an accompanying list. The items are coordinated by و: خنجر (dagger), روزبانان (heralds/announcers), و تشت (and a large basin). This lists the things/people accompanying the action.
segments.s170-g319: ببندند is a 3rd-person-plural jussive/subjunctive (commandive) 'let them bind' or 'they should bind'. دستش = 'his hand(s)' (دست + enclitic -ش 'his'). به خم کمند is a locative/instrumental phrase 'to/with the curve/loop of the lasso' (خم = curve/loop, کمند = lasso/snare). The verb governs the object دستش and the instrument phrase.
segments.s170-g320: بخوابند is another jussive/commandive form 'let them lay (him) to sleep/lay down' (here 'put him down'); the object is understood from context. بر خاک = 'on the earth/ground'. چون گوسفند = simile 'like a sheep' (i.e. laid down meekly or as a sacrificial/helpless animal).
segments.s170-g105: بران is a demonstrative/locative particle 'on/upon that' placed before the noun phrase. تشت زرین is a noun + adjective ('basin golden' = 'golden basin'); classical Persian often orders noun then adjective. کجا is a relative adverb 'where' that introduces the clause describing location, and خون اوی is a noun + possessive (اوی = his) 'his blood'.
segments.s170-g106: فرو ریخت is a two-part verbal expression: فرو (adverb) 'down' placed before the verb ریخت (past of ریختن) 'poured/flowed down'. ناکاردیده is a past-participle-like adjective 'inexperienced/untried' modifying the following agentive گروی; گروی is a poetic/archaic agentive form from گرویدن meaning 'one who has joined/adopted' (here used as 'follower/retainer' or 'one who performed the action'), so the phrase functions roughly 'poured down by an inexperienced follower.'
segments.s170-g107: بمالید here is the verbal kernel 'rub' in a classical/poetic form used with the auxiliary خواهم; in classical Persian the auxiliary (خواهم 'I will') can precede the verb form. همی is an older habitual/continuous particle ('still/continually') placed near the verb; روی و چشم are the direct objects 'face and eyes.' The order (auxiliary + particle + verb + objects) is typical of older poetic usage.
segments.s170-g108: مگر introduces a conditional/exceptive clause 'unless'. بر دلم is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on my heart' (بر + دل). م is a vestigial/archaic clitic here tied into the verbal complex in poetry (a reduced present/copying particle related to 'می' or an emphatic copula); the main verb is شود 'become' (present/subjunctive sense). کم modifies شود: 'become less/fewer.' درد و خشم are the coordinated nouns 'pain and anger' that are the semantic subject (or theme) of 'become less' in this construction.
segments.s170-g109: وگر is the conditional 'if'. همچنانم is an adverbial/pronominal phrase 'I am still thus / still so' (همچنان with the enclitic -م for 'me/I' in archaic usage). بود is the copula 'were/was' (classical past/irrealis sense in a conditional). بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound/tied' modifying چنگ; چنگ here is used poetically as 'claw' or figuratively for 'hand/grip' (context decides), so the phrase reads 'bound (with) claws' or 'with claws bound.'
segments.s170-g110: نهاده is a past-participle 'placed/put'. It takes the following locative phrase به گردن 'on/at the neck' (به here = 'onto/at'), and درون پالهنگ 'inside the leopard' specifies the location more narrowly (درون = 'inside/within', پالهنگ = 'leopard'). The structure is participle + locative phrase: '(having been) placed on the neck within the leopard.'
segments.s170-g111: به خاک اندرون is a prepositional-locative phrase: به ('into/toward') + خاک ('earth') + اندرون ('inside/within') = 'into the earth.' خوار is an adjective after the noun meaning 'despised/abased' and functions adverbially here ('disgraced'). چون گوسفند is a simile 'like a sheep.' The word order (preposition + noun + postpositive adjective + simile) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s170-g112: کشندم is an older verbal form meaning 'they kill/drag me' (کشند + enclitic -م = 'me'); in context it reads as a verbal clause about what 'they' will do to the speaker. دو بازو ('two arms') is the direct object (my two arms). به خم کمند is a locative/instrumental phrase: به ('into/on') + خم ('curve/bend') + کمند ('lasso/snare'), i.e. 'into the bend/loop of a snare.' The verb first then object/order is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s170-g57: از is the preposition 'from'. اندیشه خرد is a genitive-like noun cluster (اندیشهٔ خرد) meaning 'the counsel/thought of wisdom' with the adjective/possessive noun خرد ('wisdom') following اندیشه. و simply links the next noun phrase; شاه سترگ names a second agent 'the king [سترگ]', where سترگ is a poetic/archaic adjective; the whole segment lists the sources/agents of what follows.
segments.s170-g58: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (literally 'arrived'). به ما marks the indirect object 'to/for us'. بر introduces the effect/target 'upon/against' and combines with زیانی بزرگ 'a great loss' (زیانی = 'loss', بزرگ = 'great', adjective after noun). Together: 'came upon us a great loss.'
segments.s170-g59: کسی 'someone' + کاو is an older/poetic relative-form meaning 'who' (ka-ū), so the phrase reads 'someone who'. بود is the past copula 'was'. مهتر انجمن is a noun phrase 'the chief/head (مهتر) of the assembly (انجمن)' with the possessor following the head noun in classical order.
segments.s170-g60: This is an elliptic comparative statement: کفن بهتر 'a better shroud' (i.e. preferable fate) + او را marks 'for him' (او را = 'to/for him' with را topicalizing/emphatic). ز here functions as 'than' in comparison (poetic use of ز = 'than/from'), followed by فرمان زن 'the command/order of a woman' (فرمان = command, زن = woman). The sense: 'A shroud is better for him than the (woman's) order.'
segments.s170-g61: سیاوش is the subject/name. به گفتار زن uses به + noun to indicate agency or cause here ('by/through the woman's speech' — i.e. on account of what the woman said). شد به باد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: شد 'became/was' + به باد 'to the wind' meaning 'was ruined/was blown away'. The verb is simple past/resultative.
segments.s170-g62: خجسته زنی is an independent nominal phrase 'a blessed/fortunate woman' (خجسته = auspicious/blessed, زنی = 'a woman'). کاو is again the archaic/poetic relative-form 'who'. ز مادر نزاد is a passive/agentless birth expression 'born of a mother' (ز = 'from', مادر = 'mother', نزاد = born/beget), so the clause reads 'the blessed woman who was born of a mother.' (The line is terse/poetic and may be ironical or formulaic.)
segments.s170-g63: دریغ is an interjective noun/exclamation 'alas/pity'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' (pointing back). The following three nouns joined by و (بر و برز و بالای او) form the things being lamented: a sequence of lofty/authority-related nouns (here rendered 'force / loftiness / his high estate'); بالای او literally 'his height/highness' (بالای = 'above/loftiness' + او 'his'). The series is a coordinated list following the exclamation.
segments.s170-g64: رکیب و خم is another coordinated pair: رکیب here means 'companions/associates' (often riders/attendants) and خم is a martial/ornamental word (literally 'curve' but used for a curved weapon or arch — 'arms/accoutrements' in context). خسرو آرای او is a compound where خسرو 'king' modifies آرای (آرای = 'adornments/ornaments'), with او 'his' closing the phrase: 'the kingly ornaments that adorned him' or 'the regalia about him.' Grammatically this is a noun phrase enumerating the lamented possessions/retinues.
segments.s170-g185: دگر here is an adverb meaning 'again' or 'then' and precedes the main verbal phrase. حمله (nominal) combines with the past verb کرد in حمله کردش: کردش is کرد + enclitic -ش (third-person object) so the whole construction means '(they) attacked him.' هزار و دویست is the subject (one thousand two hundred) placed after the verb for emphasis — classical Persian allows postverbal numerals.
segments.s170-g186: ورازاد را uses را to mark the person being addressed (object/recipient of گفت). گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'said/told' (here introducing reported speech). لشکر begins the reported clause and مه‌ایست is a poetic fused copula (مه + است) meaning literally 'is the moon' — the reported predicate follows the subject in standard Persian order.
segments.s170-g187: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/because'. امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. بادافره is a poetic compound (literally 'wind‑borne') used here as a substantive meaning fickle chance or fortune — an archaic/poetic word. ایزدیست is a fused predication (ایزدی + است) meaning 'is godly/divine'; the copula is attached to the adjective/noun.
segments.s170-g188: مکافات بد is a genitive/nominal phrase 'retribution/punishment for evil' with را marking it as definite object/topic. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and یزدان is 'God'; بدیست is the predicate 'is bad/evil' (bad + است fused) — the clause literally says 'the retribution of evil from God is evil', with the copula attached to the adjective.
segments.s170-g189: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' modifying what follows. لشکر گشن places the adjective (گشن 'hungry') after the noun in the classical order ('the hungry host'). و چندین سوار is a coordinate phrase 'and several horsemen' with چندین preceding the noun.
segments.s170-g190: سراسیمه شد is the past construction 'became frantic/was thrown into panic' (شد = became). از here functions causally 'because of / at the sight of'. یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one' and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned' modifying that one — together 'by a certain renowned man.'
segments.s170-g191: همی شد is a classical/perfective-like sequence marker here giving immediacy ('at once / straightway he...'); شد is the past 'became' or auxiliary of action. فرامرز is the subject's name. نیزه به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'spear in (his) hand' where به + noun expresses the instrument/position ('with / having the spear in hand').
segments.s170-g192: ورازاد را marks Vrazad as the direct object/affected party. راه یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the way/path of God' (راه + یزدان). ببست is the past transitive verb 'he shut/blocked' — together 'he blocked Vrazad's way (to) God.' The object (ورازاد را) appears before the verb as expected with را.
segments.s170-g161: فرامرز گفت is Subject + past-tense verb ('Faramarz said') marking reported speech. The following ای + شوربخت is a vocative ('O wretch') and گو here is an imperative/command 'speak' placed between the vocative words: literally 'O, speak, wretch.' This ordering (imperative inserted with the vocative) is a poetic, slightly abrupt address.
segments.s170-g162: منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + emphatic -م). بار is a predicate noun ('burden/responsibility') followed by the demonstrative phrase آن خسروانی درخت ('that royal tree'); note a poetic omission of the ezāfe (بارِ آن خسروانیِ درخت) — modifiers follow the head noun in classical word order. The whole phrase reads 'I am the burden of that royal tree.'
segments.s170-g163: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'so that'). از نام او means 'from/at the name of him' = 'at the mention of his name.' شیر پیچان is a nominal phrase (شیر + adjective پیچان) and شود is the copula/auxiliary 'becomes/appears' — classical Persian often uses شود to state a general consequence.
segments.s170-g164: چو = 'when' (temporal). خشم آورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'brings/takes up anger' (آورد here conveys 'cause/come into being'), so the clause means 'when he becomes angry.' پیل بیجان شود uses پیل (elephant) + predicate بیجان شود ('becomes lifeless'); again شود functions as 'becomes' in a general/resultative sense.
segments.s170-g165: The poetic string here is object (مرا) + marker (را) + prepositional phrase با تو ('with you') followed by the accusative/epithet بدگوهر دیوزاد ('ill‑gem, demon‑born') describing the agent. Note the segment shows classical compactness: م + را were split in the annotation but together function as the object 'me' (مرا). The order places objects before the agent‑epithet in a compressed, accusatory clause.
segments.s170-g166: چرا کرد is a simple past interrogative 'why did (he) do (it)?' followed by the modal باید ('must/it is necessary') which governs the following verbless clause همی نام یاد. همی is an emphatic/old particle ('indeed/just'), and نام یاد ('name + remembrance') is a compressed nominal phrase meaning 'to remember the name' — classical Persian often omits an explicit verb here, so the sense is 'the name must be remembered.'
segments.s170-g167: گو here functions as a discourse verb/marker 'it is said' (classical marker meaning 'they say' or 'reportedly'). The locative phrase پیلتن با سپاه از پس است literally 'Piltan with an army is from the rear' — از پس is an archaic compound meaning 'from behind' or 'following/behind' and است is the present copula.
segments.s170-g168: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that.' اندر جهان = 'in the world.' کینه‌خواه او is a compound meaning 'those who seek revenge/harbor hatred against him' (خواه functioning as an agentive element), and بس است ('is enough') means 'there are plenty' or 'sufficiently many.' The clause explains why the army follows: because many in the world bear him a grudge.
segments.s170-g41: چو is a temporal subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” نزدیکی شهر ایران is a noun phrase with نزدیکی (“nearness/vicinity”) governing the genitive ترکیب شهرِ ایران (“the city of Iran”), and رسید is a past-tense 3rd-person verb “reached/arrived.” Word order is typical classical Persian SOV inside the clause: temporal conjunction + locative noun phrase + verbal predicate.
segments.s170-g42: همه is the plural subject “all/everyone.” جامه پهلوی is the object noun phrase (“the Pahlavi garment(s)”), with پهلوی functioning adjectivally (a type/type-name of garment). بردرید is the past-tense verb “tore” agreeing with the plural subject; the clause follows standard SOV ordering: subject + object + verb.
segments.s170-g43: به here marks the object/goal of the oath ('by' or 'to'); دادار دارنده is a two-word epithet in apposition meaning “the Creator, the Sustainer/Holder.” سوگند خورد is an idiomatic compound meaning “swore an oath.” Note that خورد is used idiomatically with سوگند (not literally “ate”), so its literal dictionary senses do not list ‘swear’; treat it as the fixed verb of the idiom.
segments.s170-g44: که introduces the reported content of the oath (“that …”). هرگز is the negator “never.” تنم (literally “my tan/thing”) here functions as the speaker’s body/person (idiomatic possessive). بی‌سلیح is an adjective phrase “unarmed.” نبرد is the verbal element expressing motion/engagement (here: ‘go/enter into battle / be taken into action’) and is negated by هرگز; the exact classical verb-form/usage is idiomatic, so some dictionary senses supplied do not match the verbal use and are marked accordingly.
segments.s170-g45: نباشد is an impersonal negative copular/optative expression meaning roughly “may it not be / it should not be.” بشویم here is a subjunctive/passive form built on the root of شدن/شستن (contextually ‘be washed’), so the clause expresses a negative wish or statement about the head being washed. سرم را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object “my head,” and ز خاک (from/of dust) is a prepositional phrase marking the source/material. The phrasing and verb forms are archaic/poetic.
segments.s170-g46: همه is “all/everything” and here functions to quantify the following locative phrase. بر تن is a prepositional phrase “on the body,” غم is the noun “sorrow” which is predicated by بود (past copula), and سوگناک is a predicate adjective “mournful.” The clause literally reads “all, on (the) body sorrow was mournful,” i.e. “sorrow covered the whole body and was mournful.” Note the classical word order where the copula بود follows the noun phrase it links to its adjective.
segments.s170-g47: This is a two-part nominal clause joined by و. کله ترگ states a nominal equation “my head (is) a rag” (ellipsis of copula common in poetry), then شمشیر جام places two nouns in apposition or metaphor (“the sword [is] a cup”). The final منست is an archaic copular phrase (man est / ‘is mine’ or ‘it is so’ in older idiom); in classical diction it functions as the predicate. The line is highly metaphorical: head=rag, sword=cup.
segments.s170-g48: به بازو is a locative phrase “on the arm.” خم here denotes a curved thing (a bow/curve) and خام is an adjective “raw/new,” while دام is the noun “trap/snare.” The final منست again functions as the archaic copula/predicate. The whole segment lists the equipment or condition on the arm: a bent (bow) and a raw snare belong to the speaker—poetic, somewhat elliptical syntax.
